name: Bruny Island Premium Wines
description: Bruny Island Premium Wines sits in Lunawanna, at the southern end of an island that already feels remote. It holds a specific distinction: this is Australia's southernmost vineyard. That fact shapes everything here — the latitude, the climate, the decision to plant at all. Bernice Dillon, who owns and runs the property, is a direct descendant of some of the island's earliest settlers. The cellar door opens onto that history without making a performance of it.

The wines and ciders are produced entirely on-site, which on an island accessible only by ferry is a logistical commitment in itself. Over 50 medals in national and international competitions suggest the quality is real, not circumstantial. The site's cool, southerly position points toward varieties that favor a long, slow growing season — expect wines with genuine acidity and restraint rather than ripeness and weight. Specific vintage notes aren't widely published, but the medal count across multiple competitions indicates consistent output over time. The cider is worth attention alongside the wine.

Bernice and her father planted the first vines in 1998, making this not just the southernmost vineyard in Australia but the first vineyard on Bruny Island itself. That founding decision looks more significant in retrospect than it probably seemed at the time. The restaurant adds genuine substance to a visit — a father-son team of classically trained chefs runs a seasonally shifting menu built around local produce, with platters and oysters available midweek and a full dinner service on Saturday nights. For anyone making the trip to Bruny Island, skipping this stop requires a deliberate choice to do so.
pullQuote: Fifty medals from the southernmost vineyard in Australia is not an accident.

name: Caledon Estate
description: The entrance at Caledon stops you before you even reach the cellar door. A five-metre sandstone wall, built from rock excavated on-site, greets visitors at the threshold — the stonemasons' work visible in every course. Inside, the architecturally designed space opens onto uninterrupted views across the Coal River Valley toward kunanyi, with an open fireplace and seating that makes clear this was built for lingering, not passing through.

Planted in 2019 across eleven hectares of dolerite-derived soils with a northerly aspect, the vineyard produces six varieties across nine wines: Pinot Noir, Pinot Noir Reserve, Chardonnay Reserve, Cabernet Sauvignon, Pinot Gris, Riesling, Sauvignon Blanc, Rosé, and a Pinot Noir-Chardonnay sparkling. Fruit goes to Spring Vale Vineyard on the east coast, where winemaker Matt Wood — formerly of Moss Wood in Margaret River and trained under Julian Alcorso at Winemaking Tasmania — has overseen production since 2013. Vineyard management recently passed to Justin Folloso, a 2023 Young Gun of Wine winner with harvests in Victoria, California, and Burgundy behind him. The combination is serious for an estate this young.

The Stewarts bought this former sheep farm in Richmond in 2018 with no winemaking background — one a lawyer, one a teacher — and built the vineyard with direct mentorship from the Lyne family at Spring Vale, sourcing all their vines from established Tasmanian properties including Craigie Knowe and Boomer Creek. The cellar door followed in 2022. The estate's logo, drawn by their daughter at age two, sits on every bottle. With a 4.9 Google rating across 80 reviews and a food menu running Thursday to Monday, Caledon has become one of the more compelling new arrivals in the Coal River Valley — and the quality of the team now in place suggests the best bottles are still ahead.
pullQuote: The vineyard is only five years old, but the team making the decisions isn't.

name: Cathedral Rock Winery Cellar Door
description: Cathedral Rock takes its name from the rock formation that rises above the vineyard at 675 Sandfly Road, Sandfly — a specific, physical thing that anchors the property in a way few cellar doors can claim. Owners Andrea and Jamie run the operation themselves, which means the person pouring your wine is the same person who grew the grapes. The cellar door opens Thursday through Sunday, and visits often include cheese platters and live music, though the draw is simpler than that: a working vineyard in the D'Entrecasteaux Channel sub-region, one of the least-explored corners of southern Tasmania.

Cathedral Rock grows two varieties: Pinot Noir and Pinot Gris. The Pinot Noir vines were planted in 2004 from locally sourced cuttings, giving them a direct lineage to the Tasmanian viticultural tradition rather than imported planting material. The Pinot Gris followed later as the second string of the range. Production is small and estate-grown, with the wines made to reflect the cool-climate conditions of the Kingborough area — situated at roughly 185 metres elevation, overlooking kunanyi/Mt Wellington. No major competition results are on the public record, which is less a reflection of quality than of the vineyard's scale and deliberate privacy. Tasting notes and critical scores simply aren't available here; what is available is the wine itself, poured by the people who made it.

Andrea and Jamie established the vineyard in 2004, and it remains resolutely small — a handful of reviews, no distributor footprint, no Halliday listing. Cathedral Rock participates in the Southern Open Vineyards event alongside a tight cluster of D'Entrecasteaux Channel producers including Mewstone Wines and Yellow Point Vineyard, situating it within one of Tasmania's genuinely emerging sub-regions. The Google rating sits at 4.5 from 18 reviews, which is a modest sample but a consistent one. For visitors willing to leave the better-signposted Tamar and Coal River routes behind, this is the kind of place that rewards the detour precisely because it hasn't been smoothed into something easier to describe.
pullQuote: A small-batch Pinot Noir from a sub-region most Tasmanian wine drinkers haven't visited yet.

name: Clover Hill Wines
description: Clover Hill's cellar door stopped wine critic Huon Hooke in his tracks — he called it the most impressive new cellar door he'd seen on a recent trip to Tasmania. That kind of reaction doesn't come from the building alone. It comes from the full effect: the tasting experience, the small-plate kitchen run by Chef Tanya White, and the sense that every detail has been considered. Visits are by appointment, capacity is limited, and the food menu is only available when you book a tasting. That scarcity is intentional.

Clover Hill makes sparkling wine exclusively, using méthode traditionnelle with Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, and Pinot Meunier grown on ancient volcanic soils in the Pipers River subregion. Since 2019, chief winemaker Robert Heywood has been building a multi-vintage program — aging reserve wines in various vessels, then blending them with Chardonnay-dominant vintage base wines before secondary fermentation. The goal is a house style defined by freshness and consistency rather than the peaks and valleys of single-vintage releases. It pushes deliberately beyond Champagne convention rather than imitating it.

Founded in 1986, Clover Hill has spent nearly four decades making the case for Tasmania as serious sparkling wine country. The winery is owned by the same group behind Taltarni Vineyards in Victoria, but it operates with its own identity — one built entirely around the Pipers River site and what its climate and soils can do for traditional sparkling varieties. The multi-vintage philosophy introduced under Heywood marks a clear turning point: away from prestige-vintage thinking, toward a more rigorous, reproducible standard. For anyone who wants to understand what Tasmanian sparkling wine can be at its most intentional, this is the place to start.
pullQuote: The multi-vintage approach here produces something more disciplined than most single-vintage Tasmanian sparklings manage.

name: Coal River Farm
description: Coal River Farm is not primarily a winery. That distinction matters. The property runs as a working farm with a restaurant, a chocolate and cheese operation, and a shop — wine is one thread in something considerably broader. Visitors arriving expecting a conventional cellar door will find something more like a small food enterprise with a view of the Coal River Valley, where the focus is as much on what's on the plate as what's in the glass.

The farm produces wines from the Coal River Valley, one of Tasmania's warmer and drier sub-regions, which suits varieties that struggle in cooler parts of the island. Specific wine program details are limited in publicly available material, but the Coal River Valley's reputation rests on Pinot Noir, Riesling, and Chardonnay — the same varieties that define Tasmanian wine more broadly. The farm's approach to winemaking appears to sit behind its broader agricultural identity rather than in front of it, which means visitors looking for a deep technical conversation about viticulture may need to look elsewhere. What's here is honest and unpretentious.

Daniel and Melanie Leesong founded Coal River Farm after careers in executive management and law — a genuine change of direction rather than a retirement project. The early years involved vegetable growing that was largely undone by kangaroos and inadequate fencing, a detail they share without embarrassment. What grew from that start is a family-run property with a restaurant, artisan produce, and enough scale to hold a 4.4 rating across over 1,200 Google reviews. The draw for most visitors is the full experience rather than the wine list specifically — come for the cheese, the chocolate, the farm setting, and a meal. The wine is worth trying while you're there.
pullQuote: The wine is secondary here, and that's not a criticism — the farm earns its visit on other terms.

name: Coal Valley Vineyard
description: Coal Valley Vineyard keeps its cellar door to a tight schedule — closed Monday through Thursday, open Friday to Sunday — which sets the tone for how this estate operates: deliberately, on its own terms. The property sits in the Coal Valley region of South East Tasmania, a quieter corner than the Tamar Valley crowd tends to reach, and the experience here reflects that. It is a working vineyard first, a visitor destination second.

Every vine on the estate is hand pruned, every grape hand picked. The fruit never leaves the property before it becomes wine — Coal Valley operates on 100% single-site estate-grown material, which gives the range an internal consistency that blended operations rarely achieve. The flagship is the 2020 Estate Pinot Noir, priced at $58, which the winery describes as dark ruby with red cherry aromatics and plum on the palate, built to drink now or hold for up to a decade. Riesling and Chardonnay also feature in the lineup, with bundle formats that have historically sold out — a reasonable indicator of local demand. The stated house style is cool-climate classicism: no flash, no extraction, no attempt to make the wines something the site isn't.

The winery's own framing centres on sustainability and a hands-on approach, language that can mean very little but here seems to describe genuine practice rather than positioning. Single-site viticulture with manual canopy management in a cool southern Tasmanian valley is a real commitment, not a marketing decision. The cellar door also supports weddings and on-site accommodation, which gives the property staying power as a destination beyond a single afternoon visit. For anyone driving the Coal Valley corridor, this is one of the more grounded stops on the route — a place where the wines and the operation are genuinely aligned.
pullQuote: The Pinot Noir punches above its price when the vintage cooperates, and it usually does.

name: Craigie Knowe Vineyard & Flora's Restaurant
description: Craigie Knowe sits on the east coast of Tasmania as the oldest vineyard in that part of the island, and when you arrive for a tasting, you sit down — guided, unhurried, poured from proper glassware by Glenn or Alex, the family who runs the property. That's not incidental to the experience. It's the whole point.

The lineup covers five varieties: pinot noir, riesling, cabernet, pinot gris, and chardonnay. For an east coast Tasmanian producer, that's a range that leans into the region's relatively warmer and drier conditions compared to the Derwent Valley or Coal River. The cabernet is worth noting — it's a variety few Tasmanian producers persist with, and its presence here reflects a confidence in what this specific site can do. Winemaking is hands-on and small-scale, with Glenn and Alex involved at the vineyard level before fruit reaches the winemaker. Tastings can extend into wine-and-chocolate matching, which sounds indulgent but is taken seriously here.

The property has a genuine history on the east coast, and the cellar door keeps that history present — not as décor, but as something you hear about during your tasting. Glenn and Alex describe themselves as custodians of the vineyard rather than founders, which says something about how they approach the place. Flora's Restaurant operates five days a week for lunch, serving Tasmanian produce alongside the wines. Free camping is also available on-site, no booking required. For visitors driving the east coast who want something more than a quick pour and a gift shop, Craigie Knowe offers an afternoon that actually slows down.
pullQuote: The cabernet alone is reason enough to question your assumptions about what Tasmania can ripen.

name: Craigow Vineyard
description: Craigow sits in the Coal River Valley east of Hobart, one of the driest and sunniest corners of Tasmania — a fact that surprises visitors who arrive expecting the island's famously wet disposition. The property is among the earliest vineyards established in the valley, which means its vines have had decades to settle into the site. That tenure shows in the wines.

Craigow produces Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, Riesling, and Sauvignon Blanc as its core range, with a Dessert Riesling and a Sparkling Merlot NV rounding out the lineup. The Riesling is worth particular attention — the Coal River Valley's low rainfall and cool nights produce the kind of structural tension in that variety that few Australian regions can manage. The winemaking approach is squarely terroir-driven: these are wines made to reflect where they come from rather than to smooth out the valley's harder edges. The Dessert Riesling is a regional specialty that rewards the detour on its own.

Craigow was established when southern Tasmania's wine reputation was still being built, not inherited. The Coal River Valley has since developed real standing for cool-climate varieties — Pinot Noir in particular — and Craigow's early commitment to that ground gives it a legitimate claim to being part of that story from the beginning. The valley draws comparisons to cool-climate French regions, and while such comparisons are easily made, the Riesling and Chardonnay here give those comparisons enough weight to be worth considering. For visitors, the combination of a high Google rating, a small and focused range, and a cellar door experience that doesn't try to be more than it is makes Craigow a reliable and genuinely rewarding stop in the valley.
pullQuote: The Riesling alone tells you why the Coal River Valley deserves its reputation.

name: Dalrymple Vineyards
description: Dalrymple Vineyards sits on sloping ground above the Pipers River corridor, looking out toward Bass Strait. The cellar door opens only four days a week — Monday, Friday, and Saturday, eleven to four — which tells you something about priorities here. This is not a place built around foot traffic. It is built around the wines.

Pinot Noir is the central preoccupation at Dalrymple, and has been since the beginning. The estate focuses on single-site expressions of Pinot Noir and Chardonnay, the two varieties that perform most honestly in this part of northern Tasmania. The winemaking philosophy leans toward transparency — letting the character of individual parcels come through rather than imposing a house style. Cool growing seasons in the Pipers River subregion produce wines with genuine tension: fruit that doesn't tip into ripeness too early, and an acidity that gives the wines structure over time. Without specific recent vintage notes available, what the approach signals is restraint and site fidelity, both qualities that reward patience in the glass.

The vineyard was established in 1987, a period when very few producers were treating Tasmania as serious wine country rather than a curiosity. That early commitment to Pipers River — a subregion now recognised for producing some of the state's most precise cool-climate Pinot Noir — gave Dalrymple a foundation others had to spend decades catching up to. Vigneron Peter Caldwell has articulated the winery's purpose plainly: Pinot Noir, done right, is a complete reflection of its environment. Everything here is oriented around proving that point. For a visitor, the limited hours mean planning ahead, but they also mean arriving somewhere that has not been softened for convenience.
pullQuote: Thirty-five years in Pipers River shows — these wines don't need to explain themselves.

name: Darlington Vineyard
description: Darlington Vineyard sits a short walk from Prosser Bay in Orford, a town of 500 people on Tasmania's east coast. The cellar door opens Thursday through Sunday, and the unhurried rhythm of the place reflects the size of the operation — around 5,000 vines, hand-picked each April, with low cropping rates kept deliberately tight. This is not a winery that scales up.

The vineyard grows four varieties — Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, Riesling, and Sauvignon Blanc — planted in the early 1990s on a northeast-facing slope above Orford. At thirty years old, the vines now produce between seven and ten tonnes of fruit per year, modest by any commercial measure. The block's orientation allows cool air to drain downhill, moderating frost risk, while the east coast's warm summers bring fruit to full ripeness by April. The winemaking philosophy follows from the viticulture: personal attention to each vine, small berries, concentrated flavour. There is no apparent interest in volume.

The vineyard takes its name from Darlington, the convict penal settlement on Maria Island visible across the water from Orford. The town itself was established in 1825 as a staging post for convicts being transported to the island — a history that still marks the landscape. The vines were planted as Tasmania's east coast was just beginning to attract serious viticultural attention, and three decades on, Darlington has the maturity to show what that coastal site can produce. Visitors arriving outside Thursday-to-Sunday hours can book by appointment. The drive from Hobart — an hour and fifteen minutes northeast along the Tasman Highway — is reason enough to plan ahead rather than show up hoping for the best.
pullQuote: Thirty-year-old east coast vines at this scale are rare; the Pinot Noir shows exactly why that matters.

name: Delamere Vineyard
description: Delamere is planted on a steep, cold hillside in Pipers River, at 7,500 vines per hectare on deep red Ferrosol soils, and every vine is managed by hand. There is no irrigation. The fruiting wire sits just 60 centimetres off the ground, close enough that the soil's reflected heat becomes part of the ripening equation. This is a working vineyard first, and the cellar door — open Thursday through Monday — exists on those terms.

Delamere makes only two things: Pinot Noir and Chardonnay. The vineyard carries a considered mix of clones — older plantings of 8048, MV6, and Penfolds 58, alongside newer Dijon selections chosen for lower yields and smaller berries. The approach in the winery follows from the vineyard: low yields, naturally balanced vines, and fruit from blocks now old enough to produce wines with genuine structure. Pipers River's unusually low diurnal temperature range preserves aromatic detail that warmer growing regions simply cannot replicate, and the acidity in Delamere's wines reflects that directly.

Shane Holloway came to Tasmania through marine biology and stayed for winemaking, completing his postgraduate degree at Adelaide University and working vintages in the Yarra Valley, Adelaide Hills, and Oregon before committing to Pipers River. His co-owner and co-winemaker Fran Austin arrived from Western Australia by way of Burgundy, Oregon, and the Yarra Valley, drawn by what she describes as unparalleled fruit quality. Her credentials are precise: 2005 Gourmet Traveller Young Winemaker of the Year, 2009 Len Evans Tutorial Scholar, 2016 Don Martin Fellow. She joined Delamere in 2011, and the two have run it together since. A 2013 expansion brought the vineyard to 12 hectares. For visitors, the draw is simple: two winemakers with serious international experience chose this specific hill, in this specific region, and have spent years proving why.
pullQuote: The Chardonnay in particular shows what Pipers River can do when the site is taken seriously.

name: Derwent Estate Vineyard
description: The cellar door at Derwent Estate operates out of Rathbone Cottage, a building dating to around 1820, which sits on the estate grounds beside the Derwent River along the Lyell Highway at Granton. That kind of age is unusual in Australian wine country, and the cottage earns its setting. Tastings run to twelve wines, and visitors can take them beside the fire or out on the verandah. The property has a working feel to it — vines, river, historic structures — rather than the manicured aesthetic of a purpose-built visitor centre.

The estate's wine club and cellar door program suggest a range broad enough to carry a twelve-wine tasting flight, which points to serious variety across styles. Tasmania's cool climate along the Derwent Valley is reliably suited to Pinot Noir and Riesling, and properties at this latitude tend to produce whites with genuine acidity and reds that reward patience. The wines have received awards, though specific medals and vintages aren't publicly detailed in available materials. What the estate does make clear is that the cellar door experience is built around the wines rather than around spectacle — the tasting is the point.

The estate sits at Granton, roughly twenty minutes from Hobart, in one of the oldest settled parts of Tasmania. The Rathbone Cottage cellar door anchors the property in genuine colonial history rather than borrowed atmosphere. The Shed restaurant, open Wednesday through Sunday, runs a locally sourced menu matched to estate wines and operates within the same historic precinct. For visitors coming up from Hobart, Derwent Estate offers something the city-adjacent wineries rarely manage: a sense that the place existed before the wine tourism did.
pullQuote: Twelve wines in a cottage built in 1820 — the history here isn't decorative.

name: Devil's Corner Cellar Door
description: Devil's Corner sits on Tasmania's East Coast at Apslawn, where the Freycinet Peninsula frames the view across Moulting Lagoon. The cellar door is built to make the most of that position — the deck looks out over the water, and on a clear afternoon the light off the lagoon does most of the decorating. It is one of the more visually striking stops on the East Coast wine trail, and the kitchen running until 4pm means there is a reason to stay longer than a quick tasting.

The focus here is on cool-climate varieties that suit the East Coast's maritime conditions: Pinot Noir is the headline act, alongside Syrah and a range of whites that include Riesling, Pinot Gris, and Chardonnay. The wines are made under the Brown Brothers-owned Tasmanian operation that also includes Tamar Ridge, giving Devil's Corner access to significant production infrastructure without losing site specificity. The style tends toward fruit clarity and freshness over weight — the East Coast's sea breezes keep ripening slow and acid levels high, and the wines reflect that. The Pinot Noir, in particular, tends toward red-fruit precision rather than the denser, earthier profiles that can come from the Tamar Valley.

Devil's Corner is part of the Bay of Fires Wines group, acquired by Brown Brothers in 2010, which brought capital investment and broader distribution to what had been a smaller Tasmanian operation. The brand's identity is built around its specific coastal location rather than Tasmanian provenance as a generic selling point — the East Coast appellation has its own character, and Devil's Corner has been consistent in making that case. For visitors, the combination of the cellar door, the kitchen, and the water views makes it one of the more complete half-day stops in the region. Come hungry.
pullQuote: The East Coast site shows in the wines — there's a saline freshness you don't get from the Tamar.

name: Domaine A Stoney Vineyard
description: Domaine A sits on an 11-hectare north-facing slope in the Coal River Valley, planted at 6,000 vines per hectare in the European close-planting tradition. The soils run down to 200-million-year-old Jurassic dolerite. Annual rainfall rarely exceeds 500mm. Harvest stretches into late April and early May — later than almost anywhere else in Australia. The cellar door is open Thursday through Sunday, and the wines on the table are not young.

The flagship is the Domaine A Cabernet Sauvignon, an assemblage built on 90% Cabernet Sauvignon with Merlot, Cabernet Franc, and Petit Verdot, fermented in open vats with hand plunging and aged in French oak barriques. It is released only when deemed ready — sometimes after a decade in the cellar. The Stoney Vineyard Sauvignon Blanc takes the opposite approach: stainless steel fermentation, six months on fine lees, no oak. The estate also produces a Pinot Noir, Merlot, and the Petit 'a' Cabernets blend across two label tiers. The Cabernet is the wine that built the reputation, and it remains the reason most serious visitors make the trip.

The vineyard was first planted in 1973 by George and Priscilla Park — the earliest commercial planting in the Coal River Valley. Swiss-born Peter and Ruth Althaus bought the property in 1989 after searching the southern hemisphere for a cool-climate site capable of producing Cabernet on European terms. Most of the Australian wine industry thought the idea was ill-conceived. Peter held vintages back for years, sometimes longer than a decade, and released them on his own schedule. The wines earned the reputation he'd anticipated. In 2018 the Althauses sold to David Walsh, owner of Mona, and Walsh's winemaker Conor van der Reest took over with Peter's guidance before the couple returned to Switzerland in 2019. The estate is now the clearest argument Tasmania has for Cabernet Sauvignon's place in the national conversation.
pullQuote: The Cabernet proves Tasmania doesn't need warm summers to make a serious red.

name: Evenfall Winery and Restaurant
description: Evenfall sits on eastern-facing slopes above the Tamar River, where a creek along the vineyard's southern boundary feeds a natural breeze through the rows. The property has a chapel, a working winery building, and a weatherboard restaurant with large windows and a deck looking across the river toward the mountains. At the top of the ridge, tall eucalypts frame a view that takes in the whole estate at once. The name comes from an old English word for twilight — and the scene at dusk earns it.

Evenfall grows Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, and what the estate claims are some of Tasmania's oldest Cabernet vines. The winemaking is led by Liam McElhinney, with Matthias Utzinger overseeing viticulture. The 2022 Pinot Noir scored 95/100 in industry reviews — a result that reflects the site's advantages rather than luck. The eastern aspect and river influence moderate temperatures through the growing season, and the vineyard also hosts Bellebonne, a separate sparkling wine project by Natalie Fryar, widely regarded as one of Australia's foremost sparkling wine specialists. Wellington & Wolfe Riesling rounds out the on-site producers. The restaurant operates Wednesday through Sunday, pairing the wines with food in a setting that's sharp without being precious.

Evenfall is Richard Winspear's first wine venture, established when he purchased the property in 2019. Winspear grew up in Tasmania in a pastoral family and built the project around a small founding team that includes long-term collaborators Ben and Vanessa Gourlay and Magnus McGlashan. The ambition from the start was explicit: wines with international standing, not just regional interest. It's a young estate making a confident claim. The 2022 scores suggest it has the vineyard to back that up. For visitors, the combination of the restaurant, the multi-label tasting experience, and the sheer coherence of the site makes Evenfall one of the Tamar Valley's most complete stops.
pullQuote: The 2022 Pinot Noir at 95 points is the clearest sign yet this site is serious.

name: Every Man & His Dog Vineyard
description: The name does the first work here — there's a lightness to Every Man & His Dog that most cellar doors don't attempt. The tasting room pairs its wines with handcrafted chocolates made on-site by Federation Artisan Chocolate, and that pairing isn't an afterthought. It shapes the whole visit. You come expecting wine; you leave having had something closer to a long afternoon.

The winery's most distinctive offerings are its Siegerrebe and Ehrenfelser — two German-origin white varieties rarely encountered in Australian wine, let alone Tasmanian. These are cool-climate grapes that reward the island's short, cold growing seasons, and their presence here suggests a winemaker more interested in what the site can do than in making what sells easily. The Cabernet Sauvignon, available across multiple vintages including 2019, 2021, and 2023, sits at $59–63 per bottle — a range that lets visitors compare across years and see what the variety is doing in Tasmania's hands. The style across the lineup reads as small-batch and deliberate, without the volume-driven smoothing-out that happens at larger operations.

With a Google rating of 4.8 from over 350 reviews, the reputation here is built on experience as much as the liquid in the glass. The venue hosts weddings and private events, but the daily chocolate experiences run by Federation Artisan Chocolate give even a standard cellar door visit some structure. For visitors who want something genuinely different from the standard Tasmanian Pinot Noir circuit, the presence of Siegerrebe and Ehrenfelser alone is reason enough to make the detour. The cellar door is open five days a week, closed Wednesday and Thursday.
pullQuote: Siegerrebe in Tasmania is a genuine rarity — this is one of very few places you'll actually find it.

name: Freycinet Vineyards
description: Freycinet Vineyard sits on the Cosgrove property near the Freycinet Peninsula on Tasmania's east coast — the first commercial vineyard this part of the island ever produced. The cellar door is open every day, and the people pouring the wine are, in many cases, the same family that cleared the land and planted the first vines in 1979. That continuity is felt the moment you arrive.

Freycinet produces Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, Riesling, and Pinot Gris, with the cooler east coast climate lending the wines their characteristic structure and restraint. The winemaking has always been handled with a light hand — the aim, consistent across generations, is to let the site speak rather than impose on it. The vineyard earned national wine show recognition early, and the reputation has held. Without a high-intervention philosophy, what ends up in the bottle tends to reflect exactly where it came from.

Geoff Bull arrived in Bicheno in 1975 as an abalone diver — the first non-Tasmanian to hold a license for Tasmanian abalone fishing. Three years later, he and his wife Susan acquired the Cosgrove property. With guidance from Andrew Pirie, Graham Wiltshire, and Claudio Alcorso, they produced their first wines in 1983. Today the vineyard is run by their daughter Lindy and her partner Claudio Radenti, with a third generation — Giorgio, Pino, and Pia — already working alongside them. That the east coast of Tasmania has any wine industry at all owes something to what the Bulls started here, and visiting Freycinet is the clearest way to understand that.
pullQuote: The Pinot Noir shows you exactly what the east coast can do when nobody interferes.

name: Frogmore Creek Cellar Door & Restaurant
description: Frogmore Creek sits in the Coal River Valley, twenty minutes from Hobart, where the light off the water and the proximity to the city give it a character that feels less remote than most Tasmanian estates. The cellar door and restaurant share a building that opens onto the vineyard, and the Barrel Room — with its high ceilings and open fireplace — functions as a genuine events space rather than an afterthought. It is one of the few Tasmanian wineries where the food operation is equal in ambition to the wine.

The Coal River Valley's dry, continental-leaning climate suits aromatic whites and cool-climate sparkling, and Frogmore Creek has built its reputation largely on Riesling, Pinot Gris, and Chardonnay, alongside a serious sparkling program. Their Eddystone Point label extends reach into fruit sourced from across Tasmania's north and east. The winemaking approach favors precision over intervention — the goal is to keep fruit character intact through a climate that does much of the work. Multi-award winning is a phrase they use themselves, and the record supports it.

The property has been through several ownership phases since the vineyard's establishment in the 1990s, and the current incarnation reflects significant investment in both hospitality infrastructure and vineyard management. What distinguishes Frogmore Creek from comparable operations is the combination: a restaurant with real culinary intent, a cellar door that handles serious visitor numbers without feeling impersonal, and a wine range that earns attention on its own terms. The 4.6 Google rating across nearly a thousand reviews reflects consistency, which in tourism is harder to maintain than a single excellent experience. For visitors making a single day trip south from Hobart, it is the most complete stop in the Coal River Valley.
pullQuote: The Riesling alone justifies the drive; everything else is a genuine bonus.

name: Ghost Rock Wines Tasmania
description: Ghost Rock sits east of Devonport on the North West coast, a property that was run-down when Cate and Colin Arnold bought it in 2001 and has been rebuilt, vine by vine, into one of the region's more quietly confident operations. The cellar door and eatery are run as a single, unhurried proposition — open every day, no appointment needed, the kind of place where the visit itself is the point.

The cool maritime climate of the North West shapes everything Ghost Rock produces. Pinot Noir and Pinot Gris are the anchors of the range, alongside Chardonnay and Riesling — varieties that reward the region's long, slow ripening seasons. The winemaking sits closer to the restrained end of the spectrum: the fruit is asked to do the talking rather than the winemaking process. Without a swag of published scores to cite, what the range consistently reflects is a place-specific character that separates it from the output of Tasmania's more southerly producers.

The Arnold family has owned this land since 2001, but the business changed shape meaningfully in 2017 when Cate and Colin's son Justin and his wife Alicia took full ownership. Justin handles the winemaking; Alicia runs the cellar door and eatery. That division of labour shows in the coherence of the experience — nothing feels bolted on. The winery's name comes from a local piece of nineteenth-century larrikin history: a rock on the Port Sorell road where pranksters once spooked passing horsemen in white sheets. The rock is gone, flattened in a 1950s road realignment, but the name keeps the story alive. With a 4.6 Google rating across more than 350 reviews, the consistency of the visitor experience is not in question. Make the trip for the Pinot and stay for lunch.
pullQuote: Ghost Rock is what happens when a family actually commits to a difficult cool-climate site long enough to understand it.

name: Glaetzer-Dixon Family Winemakers
description: Glaetzer-Dixon operates out of a converted ice factory on the edge of Hobart's CBD — not a vineyard estate, not a rural property, but an urban cellar door that reflects how this winery actually works: sourcing from roughly a dozen growers across the Coal River, Upper Derwent, and Tamar valleys, then making the wine with as little interference as possible.

Nick Glaetzer uses no additions and only naturally occurring yeast, keeping sulphites below 50ppm. The rieslings are harvested on acid and phenolic structure rather than sugar — a method he adopted working in Germany's Pfalz — and spend six to thirty months on lees, with wild malolactic fermentation adding complexity along the way. The pinots draw on whole-bunch and carbonic maceration techniques, matured in French oak from Burgundy cooperages. The shiraz, often picked as late as mid-May, holds the white pepper character that defines cool-climate Tasmanian examples; the Mon Père bottling is aged in Cognac and Rhône Valley barrique. That wine made history in 2012 when the 2010 vintage became the first Tasmanian wine to win the Jimmy Watson Memorial Trophy, Australia's most prominent wine award.

Nick comes from a winemaking family with roots in the Barossa Valley going back to the 1850s, but he left for Tasmania in 2005 specifically for cool-climate shiraz and pinot noir. Before founding Glaetzer-Dixon in 2008, he had worked in Margaret River, the Hunter Valley, Burgundy, the Rhône, and the Pfalz — training that shows clearly in the winery's technical precision and restraint. In 2011 Gourmet Traveller Wine named him Young Winemaker of the Year; in 2019 the WSET listed him among its Future 50 at the International Wine & Spirit Competition in London. A 12-hectare estate vineyard planted at Tea Tree in 2018 will produce its first single-vineyard releases in 2026, giving visitors a forward reason to keep paying attention. The cellar door is open seven days.
pullQuote: The Mon Père shiraz is the reason Tasmania gets taken seriously as a cool-climate red wine region.

name: Glendale Vineyard
description: The cellar door at Glendale is an old apple packing shed. That fact matters. The building still carries the logic of its former life — timber framing, barn proportions, the kind of structure built to do work rather than impress. Coming down the long gravel road toward it, through Tamar Valley farmland, you understand this is not a place that has been designed for you. It existed before wine tourism was a consideration, and that shows in the best possible way.

Glendale's winemaking philosophy is stated plainly on their own terms: nurture the vine, let the fruit speak. In the Tamar Valley, that's a credible position. The long, cool growing season here builds slow phenolic development and retains natural acidity, giving the fruit the kind of concentration that doesn't need to be manufactured in the winery. The specific varieties in production are not publicly detailed, but the Tamar's track record points to Pinot Noir and Riesling as the region's most articulate voices, and Glendale's emphasis on vine health and balance suggests wines made with restraint rather than intervention. Visitors tasting at the cellar door will find a small range produced without the volume ambitions of a commercial operation.

The property's history is agricultural before it is viticultural — orchards preceding vines, the land carrying multiple chapters. The Orchard Shed, now used as a wedding venue, was constructed as an apple packing facility and has been restored without losing its original character. That continuity gives Glendale a grounded identity that many newer Tasmanian wineries are still building toward. With a 4.7 Google rating across 42 reviews and cellar door hours limited to Friday through Sunday, this is a place that doesn't overextend itself. For visitors, the reason to make the drive is simple: a property with genuine history, wines made without pretension, and a tasting experience that hasn't been packaged into something it isn't.
pullQuote: The old shed sets the tone — honest fruit, no performance.

name: Gorge Scenic Chairlift
description: The Gorge Scenic Chairlift is not a winery. It is a tourist attraction operating out of Cataract Gorge in Launceston, carrying visitors over the gorge on what is one of the longest single-span chairlifts in the world. There are no vines here, no cellar door, no wines to taste.

Because this is not a wine producer, there are no grape varieties, vintages, winemaking philosophies, or cellar door releases to describe. Including it in a wine tourism guide would mislead visitors expecting a tasting experience. The operation's publicly stated focus is accessible tourism and visitor experience at a natural landmark — commendable on its own terms, but outside the scope of this platform.

The Gorge Scenic Chairlift has a strong public reputation — 4.6 stars from over 1,700 Google reviews — and Cataract Gorge itself is a genuine draw for anyone spending time in Launceston. If you are building an itinerary, it pairs well with a visit to nearby wineries in the Tamar Valley. But the chairlift belongs on a different list.
pullQuote: Worth your time in Launceston — just not for the wine.

name: Grape Food and Wine Bar
description: Grape Food and Wine Bar sits in Salamanca, one of Hobart's most historically dense precincts, with heritage sandstone walls that predate most of the wine list by a century or two. The space leans into its industrial fit-out rather than fighting the bones of the building, and the result is a bar that feels genuinely urban in a city that doesn't always manage that. On Friday and Saturday nights it runs until 4:00 AM — which tells you something about what kind of place this is.

The wine list is the main event. Grape makes no apologies about positioning itself as a destination for serious drinking, and the list skews heavily Tasmanian, which means Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, Riesling, and sparkling wines from the island's cooler subregions get proper representation. The tapas-style food menu is built to keep pace with extended wine sessions rather than anchor them — small plates, enough variety to hold a table for hours. The cocktail program is house-original, which is either a selling point or a red flag depending on your priorities.

Grape occupies a particular niche in Hobart's wine scene: it's a bar first and a wine destination second, which sets it apart from the cellar-door experience most visitors arrive expecting. With 781 Google reviews averaging 4.1 out of 5, it holds its own against stiff competition in a precinct full of options. The live music programming adds another layer, though it does mean the room can shift from wine-focused to general nightlife depending on the hour. Visitors who want to work through a serious Tasmanian Pinot selection in a room with some atmosphere — rather than in a tasting room on a country road — will find Grape earns the detour.
pullQuote: The Tasmanian wine list is serious enough to justify a long afternoon here.

name: Hartzview Vineyard
description: Hartzview sits on 125 acres at Gardners Bay, in the southern Huon Valley, where heritage-listed fruit pickers' huts built in 1934 still stand on the property. The tasting room operates six days a week, and the sense of a working farm — not a curated visitor experience — stays with you throughout.

Pinot Noir was the founding ambition here, with vines first planted in 1988 after soil testing suggested meaningful parallels with Burgundy and Bordeaux. But it is the fortified wines that have come to define Hartzview's identity. For over two decades, Rob and Anthea Patterson have made fruit-based fortifieds using Tasmanian small fruits — blackcurrant, blackberry, cassis, apple — with no preservatives, artificial flavours, or colourings. The range runs from a Spiced Apple Mead Liqueur to a tawny-style Blackcurrant, styles that sit outside the standard Tasmanian wine narrative almost entirely. Whether that makes them eccentric or quietly ahead of the curve depends on your palate.

The property has been in the Patterson family since 1976, when Rob's mother June purchased the land. She gifted a portion of it to Rob and Anthea as a wedding present, prompting their move from Sydney. Through the early 1980s the farm was known for gooseberry production — at one point supplying a significant share of the national market — before Rob turned his attention to viticulture. The next generation is now learning the vineyard alongside them. That continuity of family and place is not incidental to the wines; it explains why Hartzview has pursued its own direction rather than following the cool-climate Pinot template that dominates the region. Visitors who come expecting a conventional Tasmanian winery experience will find something more idiosyncratic, and more interesting, than they anticipated.
pullQuote: The fruit fortifieds are unlike anything else being made in Tasmania right now.

name: Heriots Point Vineyard and Boutique Accommodation
description: Heriots Point opens only on Sundays — a single afternoon each week, 11:30 to 5:00, on a working river farm along the Huon. That restriction is not incidental. It tells you what kind of place this is: small, unhurried, and run on its own terms.

Pinot Noir makes up ninety-five percent of the plantings here, with the remaining five percent split between Chardonnay and Sylvaner — an unusual trio for the Huon Valley, and a sign that the Rolleys are thinking carefully about what this particular site can do. The cool, maritime influence of the river corridor shapes the fruit: expect Pinot that leans toward structure and restraint rather than immediate richness. With a production this size, there are no flagship ranges or tiered labels to navigate — what they grow is largely what they make.

The land at Heriots Point has been farmed continuously for roughly 180 years, passing through three families — the Heriots, the Walkers, and now the Rolleys. Before Europeans arrived in the 1830s to clear the river banks for orchards and vegetables, the Huon River was a food source for southern Aboriginal communities over thousands of years. The original apple plantings gave way to cattle grazing, and eventually to vines. That kind of slow, accretive history shapes a place in ways that are hard to fake. The Sunday-only cellar door, the near-total focus on Pinot, the working farm surroundings — all of it points to a producer more interested in getting the wine right than in expanding reach. For visitors willing to plan around the single weekly window, it offers something genuinely rare in Tasmanian wine tourism: a place that has not been curated for an audience.
pullQuote: One day a week, one grape variety — Heriots Point does not chase volume or visitors.

name: Highclare Estate Wine Room
description: The land that became Highclare Estate sat unfarmed for more than 35 years before Matt and Sarah Hurst found it in 2016. The grass was tall enough to obscure the view entirely. They bought it anyway. The Wine Room, open daily from noon, is the public face of a property that was cleared and planted almost entirely by the Hursts themselves — a detail that becomes apparent the moment you understand the scale of what they've built: 33,000 vines on a single-origin site certified under the Tasmanian Mark.

Highclare produces Sparkling, Pinot Gris, and Pinot Noir from their first commercial vintage in 2023, with both the Sparkling and Pinot Gris earning 93 points from James Halliday on debut. The Pinot Noir from that vintage was held back for additional bottle age before assessment — a decision that says something about their priorities. Looking ahead, 2025 releases include a 100% Chardonnay Blanc de Blancs, a Sparkling Rosé, a Pinot Noir from clone d5v12, a reserve Pinot Noir from clone 114, and a Sparkling NV. The clone-level specificity signals genuine attention to what the site can express rather than what the market expects.

Matt Hurst is a town planner by trade, Sarah an advertising executive with a background in interior architecture — neither came from viticulture. What brought them from a Sydney terrace house to a landlocked Tasmanian hillside was a combination of map obsession, real estate browsing, and a clear-eyed read of what this particular aspect could produce. The estate carries Tasmanian Mark certification, confirming that everything in the bottle was grown and made on the island. For visitors, the extended Friday and Saturday hours — open until 10:30 PM — make Highclare one of the few Tasmanian cellar doors worth planning an evening around.
pullQuote: Two 93-point scores on your first commercial vintage is not a coincidence — the site is doing real work.

name: Holm Oak Vineyards
description: The working dogs at Holm Oak are named Pinot Senior and Pinot Junior. That detail tells you something. This is a Tamar Valley vineyard run by two people who take the work seriously but don't take themselves too seriously — winemaker Bec Duffy and viticulturalist Tim Duffy, who between them cover the full chain from soil to bottle. The cellar door runs weekdays only, four hours a day, which keeps things unhurried when you arrive.

Holm Oak's focus sits squarely on cool-climate Tasmanian varieties, with Pinot Noir and Riesling among their core wines. Bec brings over two decades of winemaking experience to the cellar, and her approach leans toward honest expression of the Tamar Valley rather than technical showmanship — though she isn't averse to experimenting with technique when it serves the wine. The Tamar Valley's combination of low yields and a long, cool growing season gives the fruit a precision that shows in the finished wines, particularly in the whites. Without specific recent vintage scores to cite, the 4.8 Google rating across nearly 200 reviews is at least a reliable signal that the quality is consistent and that visitors leave with a clear sense of what they came for.

Bec grew up on King Island, where her father planted a trial block of vines when she was fourteen — a small decision that eventually pulled her into winemaking. She returned to Tasmania in 2006 to establish Holm Oak. Tim arrived the following year, bringing third-generation grape-growing instincts and agronomic training, and expanded the vineyard from six to fifteen hectares. That combination — a winemaker shaped by Tasmania from childhood, a viticulturalist with deep roots in the vine — gives Holm Oak a coherence that shows in the wines. The cellar door is only open five days a week, so plan accordingly, but that constraint is part of the point.
pullQuote: Holm Oak makes Tasmanian Riesling that doesn't need any introduction beyond itself.

name: Home Hill Winery
description: Home Hill sits in the Huon Valley, on land that once grew apples. That agricultural past isn't incidental — it explains something about the place. The Bennetts didn't arrive here as winemakers. They arrived as farmers, and the vineyard grew out of that, six rows at a time.

The focus at Home Hill is Pinot Noir, and the Huon Valley gives them good reason to concentrate there. Tasmania's cool climate and long ripening seasons build the kind of acidity and structure that Pinot demands, and Home Hill has earned recognition as one of Australia's better sources for the variety. The estate covers six hectares, and cropping levels are kept deliberately low — a decision that shapes the wines more than any single technique in the cellar. Beyond Pinot, they produce Chardonnay and a small range of other cool-climate varieties. The wines have drawn national attention over the years, sitting comfortably in conversations about quality Tasmanian Pinot without needing to shout for a place at the table.

Terry and Rosemary Bennett started with a hobby farm and no particular plan to be winemakers. Twenty-seven years later, the operation is genuinely family-run in a way that's more than a talking point — their son Sean manages the vineyard, daughter Kelly runs the Wine Club, and Rosemary's sister and niece handle operations and marketing. The restaurant is open Thursday through Monday, and the cellar door runs seven days a week. For visitors, that combination — a working family vineyard, a kitchen using regional produce, wines made from fruit grown on the same land — makes Home Hill one of the more coherent and honest experiences the Huon Valley offers.
pullQuote: Their Pinot Noir doesn't try to be Burgundy — it tastes like the Huon Valley, which is the point.

name: House Of Arras
description: The cellar door sits on Baxters Road in Pipers River, a part of northern Tasmania that has quietly become synonymous with high-acid, cold-climate sparkling fruit. Bookings are essential here, which sets the tone: this is not a drop-in tasting room. House of Arras runs its visitor experience with the same deliberateness it applies to everything else.

The entire focus is sparkling wine made from Chardonnay and Pinot Noir grown across Tasmania's coolest sites. Winemaker Ed Carr works with extended lees aging — some wines spend a decade or more on lees before disgorgement — which produces a depth and textural complexity that separates Arras from most Australian sparkling. The trophy haul is extensive and hard to dismiss: back-to-back Sparkling Wine Trophies at the National Wine Show of Australia in 2024 and 2025, a Chairman's Trophy at the Champagne & Sparkling Wine World Championships in 2024, and a World Champion title for a library vintage in 2022. James Halliday has called Carr Australia's greatest sparkling winemaker. Tyson Stelzer has written that no Australian house is more awarded. These are not casual assessments.

Ed Carr has been the constant. His work over decades has given Tasmanian sparkling wine much of its international credibility, and House of Arras has been the vehicle. The 2015 Australian Society of Viticulture and Oenology Winemaker of the Year and a 2018 Lifetime Achievement Award from the Champagne & Sparkling Wine World Championships mark a career that has shaped what Australian sparkling can be at its best. The winery is now the official sparkling wine partner of the Art Gallery of New South Wales, a small but telling signal of where the brand sits culturally. For visitors, the case for making the drive to Pipers River is straightforward: there is no better place in Australia to understand what serious sparkling wine looks like when it has been given enough time.
pullQuote: A decade on lees is not unusual here, and it shows in the glass.

name: In The Hanging Garden
description: In The Hanging Garden is not a winery. It is a live music and cultural precinct in the heart of Hobart — and it has been listed here in error.

No wines are made or sold at this venue. The precinct hosts live music across multiple spaces including the Odeon, Cathedral, and Altar Bar, with food provided by resident kitchens operating under Queen Sheba Ethiopian, Pizzeria Pizza, and Anju Korean. Upcoming bookings include Thundercat, Flying Lotus, and Hermitude. It is open Wednesday through Sunday, with late hours on Friday and Saturday. None of this is winemaking.

If you are looking for Tasmanian wine producers, this listing should be removed or reassigned. In The Hanging Garden is a legitimate and well-regarded Hobart institution — 4.5 stars across more than 1,200 Google reviews — but it belongs in a live music or dining guide, not a wine tourism app. Including it here would mislead users and dilute the credibility of every accurate entry around it.
pullQuote: This is a music venue, not a winery — it should not be in this guide.

name: Institut Polaire
description: Institut Polaire occupies a corner of Murray Street in Hobart with the deliberate atmosphere of somewhere that knows exactly what it is. Marble surfaces, plush grey leather, and a décor built around Antarctica as a guiding idea rather than a gimmick. It opens Thursday and runs until midnight on weekends — a wine bar and brasserie that operates on its own schedule, unhurried and specific.

The wine program is the primary reason to come. Institut Polaire holds the award for Australia's best wine list under 100 wines from Australia's Wine List of the Year Awards — a credential that reflects curation rather than volume. The house wine is Domaine Simha, a Tasmanian label described by the venue as avant-garde, available by the glass in a three-wine tasting flight for $30. The broader list leans into cool-climate styles consistent with Tasmania's short growing season and high-latitude conditions. The spirits program is equally considered: Sud Polaire gin, Antarctic dry martinis, alpine amaro, sake, and single malt whisky from what they call a curated spirit library. The Sud Polaire Martini Masterclass on Sunday afternoons is a practical way into that world.

Institut Polaire is the kind of Hobart venue that could only have emerged from a city comfortable enough in its identity to take real risks. The food — scallop sashimi with white shoyu, Flinders Island abalone tonnato, handmade pasta with crab alla vodka and gochujang — reflects the same logic as the wine list: sourced carefully, assembled with a point of view. The Antarctica theme, which could easily read as affected, earns its keep through the actual produce: Southern Ocean seafood, a fermenting and curing program rooted in alpine food traditions, and an obsession with cold-climate flavour. For visitors, the cellar door offers Domaine Simha wine and Sud Polaire spirits at cellar door prices. Come on a Thursday or Friday when the room is quieter and the bar staff have time to talk.
pullQuote: The wine list alone justifies the detour — few rooms in Hobart think this clearly.

name: Iron Pot Bay Vineyard
description: The cellar door at Iron Pot Bay sits inside a 1907 cottage at Rowella, on the western bank of the Tamar River. The setting is quiet and particular — a working vineyard where the vines were planted in 1988 and have been hand-pruned and hand-picked ever since. Open only Thursday through Saturday, 11am to 4pm, it draws the kind of visitors who plan ahead and leave with more bottles than they intended.

More than half of the ten hectares is planted to Chardonnay, with Semillon, Sauvignon Blanc, Pinot Gris, Gewürztraminer, Riesling, Pinot Noir, and Pinot Meunier making up the rest. The house style leans toward unwooded whites shaped by strong maritime influence from the nearby Tamar — precise, cool, and restrained rather than rich. The standout bottle is the Blanc de Noir 'The Pearl,' a 51/49 blend of Pinot Noir and Pinot Meunier, which took gold at the 2022 Tasmanian Wine Show — the winery's first gold medal. Pinot Meunier is rarely grown as a varietal in Tasmania, and its presence here gives the range a distinctiveness that sets Iron Pot Bay apart from most of its Tamar Valley neighbours. Winemaker Ashli Mani, who holds a Bachelor of Viticulture and Oenology from Lincoln University and has worked at Josef Chromy and Meadowbank, brought the first Rosé and dessert wines into the lineup in 2017.

The vineyard was established by the Cuthbert family and is now operated by the Mani family, who have continued expanding the range while keeping the scale small and the operation hands-on. In 2023, it ranked seventh in Halliday's People's Choice Award for cellar door experiences across Australia — the only Tasmanian winery in the top ten. That recognition is not incidental. The experience here has a coherence that many larger operations lack: old vines, a focused winemaker, a building with actual history, and wines that reflect where they come from. For anyone making a serious tour of the Tamar Valley, it is the kind of stop that tends to reorder the rest of the itinerary.
pullQuote: The Blanc de Noir earned its gold medal — but the Gewürztraminer is what stays with you.

name: Isle Drive U2 Drink
description: Isle Drive U2 Drink is not a winery. It is a tour operator, and that distinction matters. What it offers is a guided day through four of southeastern Tasmania's wine regions — Huon Valley, Coal River, Derwent Valley, and the East Coast — in a single private vehicle with a maximum of seven passengers. The point is access: to small producers, multiple appellations, and the kind of itinerary flexibility that a rental car and a map cannot reliably provide.

Because Isle Drive U2 Drink moves between regions rather than producing wine of its own, the 'cellar door experience' here is plural. The tour portfolio covers stops at wineries, distilleries, cider makers, and craft brewers across southeastern Tasmania. Each of the four regional circuits has a set itinerary, but groups can redirect mid-tour if a particular venue warrants more time. The range of producers visited means the tasting experience shifts considerably depending on which tour you book — Coal River and Derwent Valley lean toward cool-climate Pinot Noir and Riesling country, while the East Coast circuit tends to bring in more diverse styles.

The business is family-owned and operated, and draws on firsthand touring experience in France, Italy, New Zealand, and South Australia alongside Tasmania itself. Tours run seven days a week, 9am to 5pm, in air-conditioned vehicles driven by licensed operators. The hard sell is simple: you do not have to think about who is driving. For visitors who want to cover serious ground across Tasmania's wine south in a single day without logistics eating the experience, this fills a genuine gap. The 4.8 Google rating across 32 reviews suggests the execution tends to hold up.
pullQuote: Four regions in one day, no designated driver problem — it's a practical solution that actually works.

name: Jansz Tasmania
description: Jansz Tasmania sits at Pipers River in the island's north, where the winery's cellar door overlooks a lake adjacent to the vineyard. It is one of the few Australian wine properties dedicated entirely to sparkling wine — no reds, no still whites — just méthode champenoise, since 1986. That single-minded focus shapes everything about a visit here.

Winemaker Teresa Heuzenroeder, with over three decades across cool-climate regions including Adelaide Hills and Eden Valley, leads production on the house's core range built from Chardonnay and Pinot Noir. The style leans precise rather than opulent — fine bead, restrained dosage, and an acidity that reflects the region's cold maritime influence. The vintage cuvées, in particular, show what Tasmanian fruit can do when it's left to drive the wine rather than be corrected by it. Jansz has earned consistent recognition in Australian sparkling wine circles, and its Premium Cuvée NV is the entry point most critics point newcomers toward first.

The connection to Champagne is not accidental. In 1986, the famed house Louis Roederer partnered with Heemskerk Wines to plant Chardonnay and Pinot Noir at Pipers River, drawn by the climatic parallels to northeast France and the region's free-draining basalt soils. The Hill-Smith Family — Yalumba's owners — purchased the property in 1998 and converted it into a dedicated sparkling house under the Jansz name. That lineage matters: Jansz is not a winery that stumbled into sparkling wine, and it shows in the consistency of the range. The cellar door is open Thursday through Monday, and the setting — lake, vineyard, and little else between them — makes the short drive from Launceston worth it on its own terms.
pullQuote: Tasmania's cold does something to Chardonnay that you can't manufacture anywhere else in Australia.

name: Josef Chromy Wines
description: Josef Chromy sits on 61 hectares at Relbia, just south of Launceston, where the cellar door and restaurant occupy a restored heritage homestead overlooking a lake and the vineyard beyond. It is a working estate that also functions as a serious dining destination, and the two things reinforce each other rather than one apologising for the other.

The vineyards run across elevations between 81 and 170 metres, planted in alkaline clay and stony soils over dolerite — conditions that push the estate's pinot noir, chardonnay, and riesling toward tension and definition rather than weight. Winemaking here is minimal intervention by intent, aimed at preserving the character of cool-climate Tasmanian fruit rather than reshaping it in the cellar. The sparkling program is a particular strength, drawing on the same varieties that made this corner of the Tamar Valley worth planting in the first place. Within four years of its 2007 founding, the estate had collected 14 trophies and 170 medals — a rate of recognition that is difficult to dismiss as circumstantial.

Josef Chromy arrived in Tasmania as a 19-year-old refugee from Czechoslovakia. He built a meat processing business, then moved into wine, helping establish Rochecombe — later Bay of Fires — along with Jansz and Tamar Ridge before founding his own estate at Relbia. He died in 2024, but the winery that carries his name remains a working expression of the values he brought to Tasmanian wine: directness, commercial seriousness, and an unsentimental respect for the land. For a visitor, the combination of a well-run cellar door, a kitchen sourcing from the estate and its surroundings, and wines that consistently punch above their price point makes this one of the more complete stops in the Tamar Valley.
pullQuote: The sparkling wines alone justify the detour from Launceston.

name: Kate Hill Wines
description: Kate Hill sits at 21 Dowlings Road in Huonville, thirty minutes south of Hobart along the Huon Highway. The cellar door is open every day, which is rarer than it sounds in a region where many producers keep irregular hours. The tasting room is small and deliberately so — private tasting experiences are offered alongside the standard counter service, giving visitors a choice between a casual pour and something more considered.

Kate Hill's focus is cool-climate Tasmanian varietals, with pinot noir at the centre of what they do. The Huon Valley sits at the southern end of Tasmania's wine geography, and the growing season here is long and cool — conditions that tend to produce wines with more restraint than fruit weight. The winery has collected awards across its range, and the pinot noir is consistently cited as the bottle worth seeking out. Tasting platters are available at the cellar door, which suggests the wines are being poured with food in mind rather than as an afterthought.

The Huon Valley is still finding its footing as a wine destination — it has the orcharding history, the scenery, and the proximity to Hobart, but the winery trail south of the city is shorter than those in the Coal River or Tamar valleys. Kate Hill has positioned itself as a reference point on that trail, and its year-round hours reflect genuine hospitality rather than seasonal tourism. For visitors making the drive south, the winery sits naturally alongside stops at Home Hill and Willie Smith's Apple Shed, but it holds its own rather than relying on the company.
pullQuote: The Huon pinot noir has a coolness to it that the Coal River wines simply don't.

name: La Sardina Loca
description: La Sardina Loca operates out of one of Hobart's oldest sandstone buildings in mid-town, with seating split between a courtyard and a converted horse stable. The bones of the place are genuinely old. The atmosphere is not trying to be anything other than a neighbourhood bar that happens to take its food and drink seriously.

The wine list draws from Tasmania, the Australian mainland, and internationally — the selection shaped less by regional dogma than by what the owners actually want to pour. They note that they occasionally make wine themselves, though the program here is fundamentally curatorial rather than production-focused. The emphasis sits on natural and minimal-intervention styles, with pet nat appearing on the cocktail and function menus as a matter of course. If you're after a single estate vertical or a deep Tasmanian producer focus, this isn't that. If you want a thoughtfully assembled glass alongside a plate of local seafood, it usually is.

The bar pitches itself as an aperitivo destination — Spanish and Mediterranean small plates, Tasmanian produce, late hours on weekends until 2am. It holds a 4.7 rating across more than 430 Google reviews, which for a late-night bar in a competitive hospitality city is not nothing. The identity here is deliberate: convivial, a little loose, more interested in the table than the cellar. That's a specific thing to be, and La Sardina Loca is consistent about it. Visitors coming for a wine tourism experience in the conventional sense will want to calibrate expectations — but those looking for somewhere in Hobart to drink well and eat well after dark will find it a reliable address.
pullQuote: The list is small, considered, and they'll actually talk you through it.

name: LARK Cellar Door, Hobart
description: LARK's cellar door sits in Hobart, open seven days a week, and functions less like a tasting room than a deliberate introduction to a distillery that rewrote Australian whisky history. The space is unhurried. Visitors are expected to slow down, pay attention, and engage with what's in the glass — not move through a flight and leave.

This is a whisky house, not a winery, and the distinction matters. LARK produces Tasmanian single malt whisky, drawing on local barley and the island's cool, clean conditions to shape spirit that ages differently than anything made on the mainland. The range spans classic expressions through to limited cask releases, and the cellar door offers access to bottles that don't travel far. Master Distiller Chris Thomson, who trained under founder Bill Lark and was named Master Distiller/Blender of the Year (Rest of World) at the 2026 World Whiskies Awards Icons of Whisky, oversees a program that has become a credible benchmark for Australian single malt. The house style leans toward depth over sharpness — spirit that rewards patience.

Bill Lark started this in 1989, not with a business plan but with a question asked on the banks of Lake Sorell: could Tasmania be captured in a single malt? Getting there required his wife Lyn to personally challenge distillation laws that had been on the books since 1839. They succeeded, and LARK became the first distillery to legally produce single malt spirit in Tasmania since that prohibition. That founding act of stubbornness set the tone for everything that followed. Bill has since been called the Godfather of Australian Whisky — a title that understates the degree to which LARK's early work made the country's entire craft whisky industry possible. A visit to the Hobart cellar door is a direct line to that origin.
pullQuote: If you want to understand Australian single malt, this is where you start.

name: Laurel Bank Vineyard
description: Laurel Bank sits on a moderate north-facing slope at Granton, twenty minutes from Hobart, looking down over the River Derwent. The orientation matters here. That aspect, combined with a cool maritime microclimate, is not incidental to the wines — it is the premise on which the whole operation rests.

The vineyard is planted to Riesling, Sauvignon Blanc, Pinot Noir, and a small parcel of Bordeaux varieties led by Cabernet Sauvignon. Low yields are the stated ambition, and the site's cool, dry summers and long autumns support that approach rather than fight it. Without published tasting notes or recent awards on record, it is the varieties themselves that speak to intent: these are cool-climate grapes planted where cool-climate conditions actually occur, not where a winemaker wishes they did. That alignment between site and variety is rarer than it sounds.

Kerry Carland established Laurel Bank in 1986, which places it among the earlier wave of Tasmanian vineyards and gives it nearly four decades of accumulated knowledge on this particular patch of ground. Carland has been consistent in arguing that site selection is the non-negotiable foundation of Tasmanian wine quality — a view that was less fashionable in 1986 than it is now. The vineyard has never sprawled. It remains a single focused site with a tight varietal program. For visitors, that focus is the point: this is not a cellar door built around an experience, it is a working vineyard with a clear point of view about what this land can do. Coming here means engaging with that argument on its own terms.
pullQuote: Thirty-eight years on one site teaches you things a winemaker cannot fake.

name: Loira Vines
description: Loira Vines has a founding story that most vineyards couldn't manufacture if they tried: the owners came on holiday to the Tamar Valley in 2017, went on a wine tour, and instead of buying a bottle, bought the vineyard. That decision — impulsive, all-in — set the tone for everything that followed. The cellar door opens Friday through Sunday, and the property sits just north of Exeter on the West Tamar Highway, where five and a half acres of vines, some over twenty years old, share the land with apple trees and hop plants.

The vineyard grows Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Chardonnay, and Shiraz, along with Fragola — an Italian grape variety with strawberry character — which finds its way into both wines and gin. Golden Delicious and Pink Lady apples go into craft ciders, and the hops supply a house pale ale. It is an unusually broad production for a property this size, and it reflects owners who are genuinely curious rather than narrowly focused. The Fragola gin in particular represents something you won't find at most Tamar Valley stops.

The name Loira comes from the Aboriginal word for 'charcoal reduced to powder' — the locality where part of the property sits. That local knowledge runs through the operation. Rather than importing a fine-dining aesthetic, the cellar door leans into its neighbourhood: the produce platters draw on cheesemakers, charcuterie producers, sourdough bakers, and growers from the surrounding area, served alongside tastings and gin paddles. Bookings are essential for the platters, which run until 2:30pm. For a small producer with a five-star Google rating across 122 reviews, there's clearly something working here that goes beyond the wines alone.
pullQuote: The Fragola gin alone is worth the detour — you won't find that grape grown just anywhere.

name: Lucinda Wine Bar
description: You pass through Lucinda to get to Dier Makr. That geography matters. The wine bar occupies a heritage room on Collins Street in Hobart — pressed metal ceilings, hardwood floors, blackwood door frames — and what began as a spillover space for the restaurant has become a destination in its own right, with over 650 bins and a by-the-glass list that changes daily, sometimes hourly, running anywhere from ten to twenty-five open bottles on a given night.

Lucinda does not make wine. It pours it — and that is very much the point. The list runs heavily toward natural, minimal-intervention producers, from grower Champagnes to obscure bottles out of the Jura and Auvergne, alongside Australian producers who rarely appear on lists this far south. A Coravin system means rare bottles get opened without being committed, which allows the list to go places most bars won't. Gourmet Traveller WINE and Star Wine List named Lucinda Best Sparkling Wine List in Australia; World's Best Sommelier Arvid Rosengren, sitting on the judging panel, praised it for covering both the classics and smaller producers across Europe and Australia with equal seriousness. There is no fixed list. Owner Kobi Ruzicka writes it up each day based on what he feels like drinking.

Ruzicka is a Melbourne-trained chef who relocated to Hobart to open Dier Makr, then found himself with nowhere to go for a drink after service. He'd wanted to name a bar Lucinda — after a Tom Waits song — for the better part of a decade. When the Collins Street space came available, the timing held. The bar opened around late 2019 and has since pulled well clear of its origins as an adjunct to the restaurant. The food offer is simple and deliberate, the wine list personal rather than encyclopedic in the conventional sense. Ruzicka has described his guiding principle plainly: nothing on the list is something he wouldn't drink himself, in the right setting. For anyone serious about what's happening in natural and low-intervention wine right now, that principle is reason enough to show up.
pullQuote: The sparkling list alone is worth the trip from anywhere in Australia.

name: Marion's Vineyard
description: The cellar door at Marion's Vineyard sits midway up the slope in Deviot, looking out over the Tamar River from the west bank. Visitors arrive to find Roman-Greco stone heads tucked among the gardens, a stand of teenage redwood trees, and a sea eagle statue presiding over the whole property. It is an eccentric place, built up over decades, and it feels like it.

Marion's produces a range that spans the expected and the unusual for Tasmania. Chardonnay and Pinot Noir form the serious core, but the winery also makes Syrah, Tempranillo, and Cabernet Franc — varieties that rarely appear on Tasmanian labels. Several are produced as single-barrel releases under the 'One Barrel Wonders' label, meaning quantities are genuinely limited. The vineyard has been fully certified organic and biodynamic, and the approach across the range reflects that: minimal intervention, fruit grown on mature vines that have been in the ground since the early years of the operation.

Mark and Marion — a Cypriot and a Californian — cleared this block of bush and rock in the summer of 1979, making them among the earliest vignerons in the Tamar Valley. Three generations of the family now work the property. That continuity shows in the vines, which are old enough to give the wines genuine depth, and in the property itself, which has accumulated its character slowly and without particular concern for conventional winery aesthetics. The cellar door doubles as a taverna delicatessen where visitors can put together a platter and sit with a view of the river. For anyone driving the Tamar Valley Wine Route, it is one of the few stops that is genuinely harder to leave than to arrive at.
pullQuote: The Tempranillo and Cab Franc are the real reason to seek this place out.

name: Mayfield Estate
description: The cellar door at Mayfield Estate faces Great Oyster Bay directly — Schouten Passage to the east, Dolphin Sands to the north, Maria Island and the Tasman Sea to the south. There is no careful framing here, no considered angle. The water is simply there, on three sides, and the wines you taste are grown a short walk away from it.

Mayfield produces five varieties from 9 hectares, anchored by Pinot Noir and Chardonnay — the original plantings, laid down in 2010 and for six years supplying fruit to a premium Australian sparkling producer before being redirected into estate wines. More recently, Pinot Gris and Shiraz have been added to the mix. The viticulture is still young by any serious reckoning, which means the wines are in the process of becoming something rather than arrived. That is not a criticism.
