East coast Tasmania, with its maritime exposure and dry growing conditions, is a compelling place to watch that process unfold.

The Dunbabin family has worked this land since 1969, originally running sheep for super-fine wool — one of the better-regarded wool-growing operations in the country. The pivot to viticulture came in 2010 after drought pushed Bruce Dunbabin to diversify. The cellar door itself only opened in 2022, which means the whole winemaking enterprise is barely a decade old and still finding its register. That relative youth is part of what makes a visit worthwhile: you are seeing a serious Tasmanian estate at the beginning of its story, not its retrospective.
pullQuote: The Pinot Noir is still finding its feet, but the site behind it is hard to argue with.

name: Meander Valley Vineyard
description: Meander Valley Vineyard is a working family property where the people running the cellar door are the same ones tending the vines. Bron and Jade moved their family here from elsewhere in 2021 — a deliberate change of life, not a retirement project — and set up on-site in Red Hills, about 35 minutes from both the Spirit of Tasmania terminal in Devonport and Launceston Airport. Dogs on leads are welcome. The hours are Wednesday to Sunday, 11 to 5. It is the kind of place that is easier to stay at than to leave quickly.

The vineyard sits in Tasmania's north, in cool-climate conditions that favour structure and restraint over ripeness. The wines are described on site as robust — which in this context likely means they carry genuine body for the latitude — and the range leans into what the Meander Valley does well: varieties that benefit from a long, cool growing season. Specific vintage notes and tasting scores are best confirmed at the cellar door, where the range is poured in full. A wine club offers discounts and free shipping for those who want to continue the relationship after the visit.

The founding story here is straightforward and worth taking at face value: a family that wanted a different kind of life, bought a vineyard, and made it work. Since 2021 they have achieved a 4.9 Google rating across 137 reviews, which is not a marketing claim but a consistent signal that visitors leave satisfied. Accommodation is available on the property through their Vineyard Escape offering, which makes a longer stay practical rather than aspirational. The Meander Valley is also part of a wider tasting trail, positioned near producers of regional food and within reach of the Western Tiers and Cradle Mountain. The draw here is the whole of it — wines included.
pullQuote: The scores reflect something real: this place is run by people who are actually present.

name: Mewstone Wines
description: Mewstone operates on its own terms. The cellar door is open four days a week — Friday through Monday — and tastings run to a guided format: seven or eight wines, $25 per person, led by someone who actually knows what's in the glass. It is a deliberate, unhurried setup, and it shows.

The winery produces two ranges: Mewstone and hughes & hughes, the latter named for the family behind the project. Both lean toward cool-climate expression — the kind of winemaking that trusts low yields and restraint over intervention. Pinot Noir and Chardonnay are the anchors, as they are across much of Tasmania's serious wine country, but the seasonal tasting menu shifts with what's ready and worth showing. The approach is minimalist without being precious about it.

Mewstone sits in the Huon Valley, one of Tasmania's cooler and more southerly growing areas, where the season is short and the fruit concentration comes slowly. The operation is small enough that the people pouring the wines are likely the people who made them, or close to it. That directness — winemaker to visitor, no intermediary — is what distinguishes a place like this from a larger operation with a tasting room staff reading from a script. The 4.8 Google rating across nearly 80 reviews is consistent with what visitors report: the experience feels considered rather than produced. Book ahead; walk-ins are accommodated where possible but seating isn't guaranteed, and the space is kept deliberately intimate for a reason.
pullQuote: The hughes & hughes Pinot Noir is one of the most honest arguments for the Huon Valley you'll find.

name: Moores Hill Estate
description: At 120 metres above sea level on a northeast-facing ridge near Exeter, Moores Hill sits at one of the coolest growing sites on the West Tamar. That cold is not incidental — it is the whole point. The vineyard is 5 kilometres from the Tamar River and 30 kilometres from Bass Strait, and on still mornings you can feel exactly what that means for the grapes.

The estate grows and bottles single-vineyard wines from fruit ripened slowly under those conditions — the kind of slow ripening that preserves acid structure and keeps aromatic intensity intact. Pinot Noir and Riesling are the natural fits for a site like this, though the range extends across varieties well suited to cool-climate Tasmania. The winemaking approach lets the vineyard do the work: these are not wines built for early impact. They are built for the table, and for time. The 4.7 Google rating across more than 200 reviews reflects a consistency that is hard to fake at this scale.

Moores Hill was established in 1997 as a family operation, and it has remained one. That continuity shows in how the property is run — open seven days a week, without the erratic hours that affect many small Tasmanian producers. The estate model, where all fruit comes from their own vines on the one site, keeps the focus tight. There is no blending across sources, no adjustment for the market. What the ridge gives in a given year is what goes into the bottle. For visitors, the cellar door is the right entry point — unhurried, with wines poured by people who understand exactly where each bottle came from.
pullQuote: The Riesling alone justifies the drive up to Exeter.

name: Moorilla Winery
description: Moorilla sits on the Berriedale Peninsula, a narrow finger of land that juts into the Derwent River just north of Hobart. It shares the site with MONA — the Museum of Old and New Art — and that proximity is not incidental. The same sensibility that built one of Australia's most confrontational cultural institutions shapes how wine is made and presented here. A visit to Moorilla is never just about the wine.

The winery produces under three distinct labels, each with a declared philosophy. Muse draws on European winemaking traditions — structured, textural wines built for the table and for time in the bottle. Praxis runs in the opposite direction: fruit-forward, contemporary, designed to be opened young. The Cloth Label Series sits above both, a small-production range of vineyard blends that represent the estate's clearest statement on what this particular stretch of Tasmanian land can produce. Across all three ranges, the approach is deliberate and internally coherent — these are not wines searching for an identity.

Moorilla was established in 1958 by Claudio Alcorso, an Italian-born textile entrepreneur who planted vines on the peninsula at a time when Tasmania was barely considered a wine region at all. He understood cold-climate viticulture before most Australians had reason to. David Walsh — founder of MONA — acquired the estate in the 1990s and embedded it within a broader cultural project that has since reshaped how the world thinks about Hobart. The winery operates Thursday through Monday, closed Tuesday and Wednesday. If you are already making the trip to MONA, skipping the cellar door would be a genuine mistake.
pullQuote: The Cloth Label wines are the most serious argument Moorilla makes, and it's a convincing one.

name: Nadine's @ Piper's Brook
description: Nadine's at Pipers Brook is not the vineyard itself — that distinction matters. It's a separate café business operating inside the Pipers Brook Vineyard winery building, run by a local operator named Nadine. She offers self-guided tastings, dine-in and takeaway bottle sales on the estate's behalf, and a menu built around fresh Tasmanian produce. The setup is informal by design: you're sitting inside a working winery, tasting wines that were made in the same building, without the choreography of a formal cellar door experience.

The wines available through Nadine's are Pipers Brook Vineyard's full range — five labels in total. Kreglinger Sparkling is made by traditional method from estate fruit and carries the weight of the Kreglinger family's European sparkling heritage. The Pipers Brook Estate Pinot Noir draws from five separate vineyard blocks, with the winemaking team deliberately avoiding a house style in favour of letting each site speak. The Riesling, sourced from vines up to forty years old, is among the estate's most age-worthy whites. Ninth Island, the entry-level label, is named after a small island sitting offshore in Bass Strait. Chief winemaker Luke Whittle's stated approach is straightforward: represent the vineyard blocks honestly, rather than engineer a particular outcome.

Pipers Brook Vineyard was established in 1974 by Dr Andrew Pirie, whose search for a cool-climate Tasmanian site capable of matching northern French growing conditions became the basis of his doctoral thesis at the University of Sydney. He found what he was looking for in the Pipers River region, and the estate he built there became one of the benchmarks for Tasmanian cool-climate winemaking. In 2001, the Belgian-connected de Moor family's company Kreglinger took majority ownership, adding another layer of European wine history to an already unusual story. Visiting through Nadine's is a low-formality entry point to one of Tasmania's most significant estates.
pullQuote: The Riesling alone is reason enough to make the drive out to Pipers Brook.

name: Nandroya Vineyard
description: Nandroya sits on a north-facing slope above the North West Bay River at Margate, twenty minutes from Hobart, with Kunanyi/Mt Wellington visible to the west and the D'Entrecasteaux Channel to the east. The cellar door is open only on weekends, which gives every visit a particular quality — unhurried, unscheduled, the kind of stop that requires some intention to make.

The vineyard covers just 1.5 hectares and grows only two varieties: Pinot Noir and Sauvignon Blanc, both planted in 1990. The Sauvignon Blanc has become the signature wine, grown at a time when the variety was barely fashionable and now producing what the winery describes as a distinct flavour profile shaped by high acid in cooler years. Harvest can fall anywhere from early April to May, when snow is already visible on the mountain — a range that tells you something real about the variability of this site. The vineyard is managed on organic principles and holds accreditation with the Global Network of Green Tourism. No volume play is possible on 1.5 hectares, so the wines reflect decisions made row by row rather than at scale.

The vineyard was first planted in 1990, making it one of the older small producers in the greater Hobart area. The project has always been family-led, and that scale shapes everything — from how the vines are managed to how tastings are conducted at the cellar door. Nandroya occupies a specific and underexplored corner of Tasmanian wine: not the Coal River Valley, not the Tamar, but the channel country south of Hobart, where the growing conditions are their own. For visitors, the 4.9-star rating across 34 Google reviews signals something beyond the wines themselves — the experience of being there, on that slope, with that view, is apparently the point.
pullQuote: The Sauvignon Blanc from this site has an acidity and restraint you won't find further north.

name: Nocton Vineyard
description: The cellar door at Nocton Vineyard sits on Colebrook Road, fifteen minutes from Hobart Airport — close enough to feel accessible, far enough into the Coal River Valley to feel like you've actually arrived somewhere. Tastings are guided and complimentary, which is rarer than it should be in Tasmania, and the format gives the wines room to be explained rather than simply poured.

Nocton's vines were planted in 1999, making them among the oldest in the Coal River Valley. Older vines produce less fruit and concentrate what they do produce, and that constraint tends to show in the glass. The estate grows all its own fruit and makes single-origin wines — no purchased grapes, no blending across regions. The Coal River Valley sits in Tasmania's driest corridor, with warm days and cold nights that suit Pinot Noir and Chardonnay particularly well. Without specific vintage notes or trophy records available, what the approach signals is restraint: a winery that has chosen depth over volume and stayed with it for over two decades.

Founded at the turn of the millennium, Nocton has had time to understand its site in a way that younger operations simply haven't. The Coal River Valley now carries genuine prestige within Tasmanian wine — and Nocton's longevity there is part of why. A 4.9 rating across 264 Google reviews is the kind of number that reflects consistent execution rather than a single exceptional visit. The cellar door is open seven days a week, which removes the planning friction that keeps many good wineries undervisited. Come on a weekday if you can — the guided tasting is unhurried, and that makes a difference.
pullQuote: Vines this old in the Coal River Valley don't need much explaining — the wines do it themselves.

name: Ogee
description: Ogee occupies a small corner on the outskirts of North Hobart — 28 seats, no more. The name comes from the ogee arch, an architectural form defined by two curves meeting at a point. That image carries through into how the place operates: a deliberate collision of Tasmanian produce with French and Italian bistro traditions, presented without ceremony in a room that feels genuinely lived-in.

The wine list at Ogee reflects the same logic as the kitchen — local first, European second, nothing superfluous. Given the restaurant's orientation toward French and Italian culinary traditions, expect Tasmanian Pinot Noir and Chardonnay to anchor the list, with cool-climate whites likely leaning toward Riesling and Pinot Gris. The selection is curated rather than encyclopedic, designed to work with the food rather than compete with it. Specific bottles change, but the editorial point of view stays consistent: Tasmanian wine, taken seriously, in a setting that earns it.

Ogee is not a winery with a cellar door — it is a neighbourhood restaurant that treats wine with the same attention it gives the plate in front of you. That distinction matters. What it offers is something rarer than a tasting room: a chance to drink Tasmanian wine as it is meant to be drunk, alongside food that speaks the same language. Open Wednesday through Sunday, with lunch service added on Fridays, Saturdays, and Sundays, it runs a tight schedule that rewards visitors who plan ahead. With a 4.8 rating across 300 Google reviews, the word is clearly out. Book the reservation before you arrive in Hobart.
pullQuote: This is what a serious Tasmanian wine list looks like when the food actually deserves it.

name: Pablos Cocktails and Dreams
description: You find Pablo's through a red door on a Hobart street, open from five o'clock Wednesday through Sunday. The name is Cocktails and Dreams, and it means what it says — this is a bar and live music venue, not a winery.

There are no wines made here, no vineyard, no winemaking program to speak of. The listing appears in a wine tourism context, but Pablo's operates as a cocktail bar and live music joint. If there is a wine list on the menu, it isn't what brings people through that red door. Jazz jams, folk acts, hip-hop nights, and late-night sets running past midnight on weekends are the actual offering. The 4.7 rating across 234 Google reviews suggests the cocktails and the music are doing the job well enough.

Pablo's describes itself as Hobart's home of live music, and the programming backs that up — multiple acts per week, genres ranging from jazz quartet to country to indie, with late-night slots starting at eleven on Friday and Saturday. Weekly jazz jams run on Thursdays. For groups of four or more, bookings are required. It is worth being clear: visitors arriving here in search of a cellar door experience will find none. What they will find is a well-regarded Hobart bar with a serious live music schedule and hours that run until one in the morning on weekends. That is a reasonable reason to visit, just a different one than a wine tourism app might suggest.
pullQuote: Pablo's is a Hobart live music bar — not a winery, but a genuinely good one.

name: Pagan Cider Cellar Door
description: Pagan Cider's cellar door operates on an honest premise: what you taste in the glass is what grew on the tree. Open six days until 4 PM and Sundays until 5, it's a compact, unhurried stop where the product does the talking — no vineyards to tour, no winemaker mythology to wade through. The rating of 4.8 from 130 Google reviews suggests visitors leave satisfied rather than charmed into submission.

All of Pagan's ciders are whole-fruit — pressed from whole apples and seasonal fruit rather than reconstituted concentrate, which remains the industry standard for most commercial ciders. The fruit comes from fourth-generation orchardists in the Huon Valley, one of Australia's most serious apple-growing regions, and the same orchard supplies every batch. No sugar is added at any stage of production. The lineup extends beyond apple to include cherry and quince ciders, both dependent on seasonal availability. The approach is deliberately modern in technique — controlled fermentation, careful blending, elimination of oxidisation — applied to straightforwardly traditional raw material. The result is ciders that are dry by commercial standards and notably consistent batch to batch.

The two people behind Pagan Cider relocated from mainland Australia over a decade ago, drawn by Tasmania's food culture and its reputation for produce of genuine quality. The name gestures loosely at a philosophy of working close to natural cycles and seasonal rhythms — not as branding, but as an explanation for why they only make cider when the right fruit is available. Founded in 2016, the operation has stayed deliberately small. That restraint is visible in the product: no artificial sweetness, no shortcuts, nothing added to compensate for inferior fruit. For a visitor, the reason to come is simple — it's one of the few places in Tasmania where you can taste what a cider made entirely from local whole fruit actually tastes like.
pullQuote: These are the driest, most fruit-honest ciders you'll find at a Tasmanian cellar door.

name: Pipers Brook Vineyard
description: Pipers Brook Vineyard sits in the Pipers River region of northern Tasmania, where volcanic soils and proximity to the Bass Strait keep ripening slow and the wines tightly wound. Founded in 1974, this is one of the oldest estates in the state — and it carries that age visibly, in vine structure and in the unhurried way the property presents itself to visitors.

The portfolio runs across four labels: Pipers Brook Estate, Kreglinger Sparkling, Ninth Island, and Pipers Tasmania. Pinot Noir and Chardonnay anchor the estate wines, while the Kreglinger Sparkling range draws on the region's natural affinity for cool-climate méthode traditionnelle. The house style leans toward restraint — wines built on site character rather than winemaking intervention. The Pipers Brook Estate tier represents the most direct expression of the volcanic terroir, while Ninth Island serves as an accessible entry point without sacrificing regional identity.

The winery was established in 1974 by Dr Andrew Pirie, whose doctoral research in viticulture helped identify the Pipers River area as one of Tasmania's most promising cool-climate sites. That original scientific rigour has shaped the estate's character ever since. The de Moor family and the Kreglinger name now carry the operation forward, linking it to a broader European wine heritage while keeping production anchored to Tasmanian fruit. At fifty years old, Pipers Brook is not a winery finding its footing — it is the benchmark against which newer Tasmanian producers are often measured. For a visitor, that history is the point: this is where the Tasmanian cool-climate argument was first made in earnest, and the wines still make it.
pullQuote: Fifty years in, Pipers Brook remains the clearest reference point for what northern Tasmanian Pinot can do.

name: Pipers Brook Winery
description: Pipers Brook sits on nearly 200 hectares in northern Tasmania — a scale that immediately sets it apart from almost everything else on the island. Most Tasmanian producers work in small quantities by necessity; Pipers Brook operates at a different order of magnitude, with volcanic soils, Bass Strait proximity, and close to five decades of accumulated site knowledge behind every decision made in the winery.

Five labels run out of this estate: Pipers Brook Estate, Ninth Island, Kreglinger Sparkling, New Certan Pinot Noir, and Pipers Tasmania. The Kreglinger traditional method sparkling is the flagship, with one expression built entirely from hand-harvested Pinot Noir — nine months in aged French oak with batonnage, then 44 months on lees at 7.5g/L dosage. It is serious sparkling wine by any measure. Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, and Riesling anchor the still wine program. Chief Winemaker Luke Whittle, who oversaw a significant vineyard redevelopment in 2016 and 2017, is explicit about his approach: the job is to represent the blocks, not to impose a house style on them. The Ninth Island label became the most widely distributed Tasmanian wine in the country — not an accident given the volume this estate can sustain without sacrificing consistency.

Dr Andrew Pirie bought this land in late 1973 after a systematic search for Australian sites that matched the cool-climate conditions of northern France — a search that became the basis of his doctoral thesis at the University of Sydney. When he and his brother David planted in 1974, most Tasmanian vignerons were still backing Cabernet Sauvignon. Pirie backed Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, and Riesling, and Tasmania's modern identity followed from that bet. Kreglinger, a Belgian family company with roots in Bordeaux through Vieux Château Certan, took majority ownership in 2001; Pirie left in 2003 with the work largely done. The winery is worth visiting for the sparkling program alone — few producers in Australia are making traditional method wines at this level of technical ambition and volume simultaneously.
pullQuote: The Kreglinger sparkling holds its own against anything made on the mainland, and most imports.

name: Pooley Wines
description: The cellar door at Pooley Wines sits in a converted Coach House on the outskirts of Richmond, flanked on one side by the Georgian convict-built homestead known as Belmont and on the other by the vines themselves. That proximity — heritage stone, working vineyard, tasting room — is not decorative. It tells you something about how seriously this place takes its own continuity.

Pooley has built its reputation on pinot noir, chardonnay, and riesling, with the Cooinda Vale and Butcher's Hill vineyards in the Coal River Valley providing fruit across the range. Winemaker Anna Pooley works with a clear emphasis on site expression — the Cooinda Vale block in particular is regarded as the source of the estate's most serious wines. Critics have taken notice: Tyson Stelzer of the Halliday Wine Companion has positioned Pooley at what he calls 'the pinnacle of Australian pinot noir, chardonnay and riesling.' Pooley also holds the distinction of being Tasmania's first and only fully accredited environmentally certified sustainable vineyard, a credential that reflects a considered approach to the land rather than a marketing exercise. Daily cheese and charcuterie boards are available at the cellar door, with wood-fired pizza served Friday through Sunday.

Founded in 1985, Pooley Wines has had nearly four decades to understand its corner of the Coal River Valley — long enough that the vineyards carry a kind of institutional knowledge most newer Tasmanian producers are still accumulating. The family name has remained on the label and the work throughout, which gives the estate a coherence that's harder to maintain than it looks. For visitors, the draw is straightforward: a cellar door where the wines, the history, and the setting all pull in the same direction.
pullQuote: The Cooinda Vale pinot noir is why serious collectors keep coming back to Tasmania.

name: Pressing Matters Cellar Door
description: Pressing Matters sits in Tea Tree, in the Coal Valley, about thirty minutes from Hobart and a short drive from Richmond. The tasting room is inside the vineyard itself — not beside it, not overlooking it, but in it. That proximity matters. What you taste here, you can see growing.

The Coal Valley is one of Tasmania's warmer sub-regions, and Pressing Matters leans into that with Riesling as a signature — made across multiple styles and residual sugar levels, a rarity in Australian winemaking. Their numbered Riesling releases, from bone-dry to late-harvest sweet, give visitors an unusually clear picture of what one variety can do across a spectrum. Pinot Noir also features prominently, as it does across the Tasmanian south, and the approach in the winery is restrained — focused on letting the fruit and site speak rather than layering in technique. The result is wines that reward attention.

The estate has built a reputation quietly, through consistent quality rather than volume. A perfect 5.0 rating across more than a hundred Google reviews suggests the cellar door experience matches the wine. Tastings are paired with small plates, which is the right call — Riesling at multiple sweetness levels asks to be eaten alongside something. Walk-ins are welcome, though bookings are encouraged, and the property is fully accessible. For visitors coming from Hobart, the Coal Valley drive is worth the detour on its own. Finding a winery at the end of it that does this much with Riesling makes it essential.
pullQuote: The Riesling range alone justifies the drive out from Hobart.

name: Puddleduck Wines
description: Puddleduck sits on Richmond Road in the Coal River Valley, one of Tasmania's driest and warmest sub-regions — a fact that shows in the wines. The tasting room draws a steady crowd seven days a week, and the operation has the feel of a place that takes its visitors seriously without performing for them. A 4.5-star rating across nearly 700 Google reviews suggests that reputation is earned.

Winemaker John Schuts came to Tasmania specifically for Pinot Noir, and the Coal River Valley gives him a distinct canvas to work with — warmer than the Tamar, more structured than some of the island's cooler pockets. Before arriving at Puddleduck, Schuts trained in Burgundy and worked at Derwent Estate, which informs an approach grounded in Old World technique applied to Tasmanian fruit. Viticulturist Andrew Hanigan, fifth generation of the family behind the property, has spent decades developing the vineyard toward consistent skin-contact quality. That level of continuity in the vineyard is rare, and it shows in the fruit the winery has to work with.

The Hanigan family has been farming this land across five generations, and Andrew has been working the vineyard since he was thirteen. That depth of site knowledge doesn't come from a textbook. Schuts arrived after his time at Derwent Estate drew him deeper into southern Tasmanian winemaking, and the combination of a family-rooted viticulturalist and a winemaker shaped by both Burgundy and Tasmania gives Puddleduck a grounding that many newer Coal River producers are still working toward. General Manager Ange Morgan leads a hospitality team that has made the tasting experience a genuine draw in its own right. For visitors doing a serious Coal River day, Puddleduck is a worthwhile anchor.
pullQuote: The Coal River terroir and Burgundy-trained instincts make for an interesting combination in the glass.

name: Riversdale Estate Winery, Cellar Door & French Bistro
description: The first thing you notice at Riversdale Estate isn't the vines — it's the UTAS radio telescope rising above the property, a structure so incongruous and so large that it reorients your sense of the place entirely. The estate sits in the Coal River Valley southeast of Hobart, a region that gets reliably dry, mild weather through its long ripening season, and that combination of geography and climate has made it one of Tasmania's more consistent wine-producing pockets. Riversdale leans into its unusual neighbour: the telescope inspired the naming of their upper-tier range, framing single-vineyard cool-climate wine as something worth pointing skyward about.

The estate grows and bottles a wider range of varieties than most Tasmanian producers attempt. Pinot Noir and Chardonnay anchor the range, alongside Pinot Gris, Sparkling, Riesling, Sauvignon Blanc, and Syrah — the last of these a relatively rare sight in this part of the world. All wines are single-vineyard, sourced entirely from the estate's own blocks. The approach to viticulture is sustainable, with no claims to certified organic or biodynamic status, but a clear emphasis on site expression over intervention. The Coal River Valley's dry autumns reduce disease pressure and allow for extended hang time, which tends to produce wines with ripeness and definition in the same glass.

Riversdale operates as a genuine estate in the older sense: the property accommodates not just a cellar door and vineyard, but a French bistro, high tea service, accommodation, and event facilities including weddings. That breadth of offering could easily tip into distraction, but the winery's 4.4 rating across more than 550 reviews suggests the parts hold together. The cellar door runs seven days a week, opening at 10am Thursday through Sunday, and the bistro gives visitors a reason to stay for a full afternoon rather than a quick tasting and a purchase. For visitors travelling the Coal River Valley circuit out of Hobart, Riversdale offers more reasons to linger than almost anywhere else on the route.
pullQuote: The Syrah alone makes the Coal River detour worth taking.

name: Rosie in My Midnight Dreams
description: Rosie in My Midnight Dreams sits inside Brooke Street Pier on Hobart's waterfront — a floating building where the MONA ferry docks and departs, and where Laurel Benson, formerly of Frank, has built an all-day wine bar that operates from early morning coffee through to Friday evening drinks. The room has floor-to-ceiling windows, communal tables, and a soundtrack drawn from disco and funk. It is casual in the way that only confident places can be.

Rosie doesn't produce wine — it curates it. The list moves between Tasmanian bottles and selected imports from Italy and France, with a deliberate interest in up-and-coming producers. Tasmanian winemaker Peter Dredge (Dr. Edge) made a rosé specifically for the bar: Rosa Rosa Rosa, a Pinot Noir and Pinot Meunier blend with just a few hours of skin contact, pale and dry with a floral edge. Dredge calls it a 'risqué little rosé.' It suits the room. Beyond wine, the cocktail program earns its reputation — the margaritas in particular are cited often enough by Time Out Tasmania that it's no longer just word of mouth.

Benson opened Rosie in the early 2020s after stints in Hobart and London, and named it partly after a DJ Shadow track and partly after her dog. The brief was straightforward: a place that works for solo diners, post-work groups, tourists waiting for the MONA ferry, and anyone who wants coffee and anchovy toast at 8 in the morning. The blackboard menu changes daily — sourdough crumpets, overnight oats, a three-cheese toastie with preserved chillies that has become a fixture. It is not a winery destination, but for anyone spending time on Hobart's waterfront, it is a considered stop that earns a longer sit than you planned for.
pullQuote: Peter Dredge made them a rosé — that tells you what kind of bar this is.

name: Rusty Roof Vineyard
description: A 180-year-old oak tree stands near the cellar door at Rusty Roof Vineyard, and it has already hosted a music festival. That detail tells you something about how this place sees itself — less as a shrine to viticulture, more as a working property with room for people. The rusty roof of the name is not a metaphor. The farm animals are real. At 870 Castra Road in Spalford, about ten minutes inland from Ulverstone, the setting is quiet agricultural country, the kind that most visitors to Tasmania drive past on the way to somewhere more famous.

Rusty Roof only opened its cellar door in November 2025, which means critical scores, published varietal notes, and competition results do not yet exist. What the vineyard does have is a location in north-west Tasmania — a stretch of the island that receives far less attention than the Tamar Valley or Coal River, and whose producers tend to work in smaller volumes with less commercial pressure. Tasting flights are paired with local platters. Whether the wines lean toward Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, or the cool-climate Riesling and Pinot Gris that suit the region, no published source has yet documented the specifics. That story is still being written, and honestly, that is part of the draw.

Tim Walker appears to be the founder, and the November 2025 opening — marked by a lineup that included Dave Gleeson of The Screaming Jets and Tania Doko of Bachelor Girl performing under that oak — suggested someone who wanted the launch to feel like an event rather than a soft opening. Rusty Roof is a new entrant in a sub-region that has largely stayed under the radar of wine media and awards circuits. For visitors combining a trip to Cradle Mountain with something genuinely off the standard trail, this vineyard offers a reason to detour through Spalford — and a chance to taste wines before the reviewers have caught up with them.
pullQuote: Come now, while it's still something only locals know about.

name: Ryelands Farm & Vineyard, Tasmania
description: The 1908 timber farmhouse at Ryelands is the first thing you notice. It has stood at Macquarie Plains, 450 metres from the Derwent's edge, for over a century, and the property still feels like a working farm first and a vineyard second. Derek Jones's small north-facing plot slopes toward the river, draining cold air fast enough to blunt the worst frost risk. That detail matters here — it's the reason wine is made at this address at all.

Ryelands produces Pinot Noir in quantities that barely qualify as commercial. Early vintages — Derek's first batch failed outright — gave way to more assured results, with the 2015/16 vintage marking a turning point. Production remains small-batch and largely private, shared between Jones and a small circle rather than sold widely. What reaches Hobart's restaurant tables tends to arrive as limited-edition bottles or under contract for specific menu needs. No formal competition medals appear on the record, which is consistent with a winemaker whose output has never been oriented toward judges. The wines are site-specific by necessity: there isn't enough volume to blend away what the land delivers.

Jones grew up in Launceston, spent years away, and came to the Derwent Valley without a winemaking background. He learned by doing and, by his own account, by leaning heavily on neighbours already working in the industry. The farm's reputation rests as much on its produce as its wine — particularly its Mangelwurzel, a root vegetable that drew the attention of cooking school operators Sally Wise and Rodney Dunn, and its seasonal vegetables, which appear on menus at Woodbridge on the Derwent. Ryelands is not a destination for tasting-room visitors in the conventional sense. It rewards the curious traveller who wants to understand how a single 15-acre property, farmed by one person, can thread itself through Hobart's best kitchens.
pullQuote: The wines exist almost outside commerce — which is exactly what makes them interesting.

name: scholé
description: Scholé occupies a former confectionary shop on Liverpool Street in Hobart — Ivy Preddis' original one — and the bones of that history are still present in the room. It opens five nights a week, never before late afternoon, and closes at midnight on its busiest nights. The communal table seats ten. The pace is deliberate.

The wine list here is not a catalogue of prestige labels. It reads, as the team puts it, like an inventory of old friends — producers who work organically and ferment spontaneously. Sake sourced by Matt Young and Black Market Sake sits alongside two considered cocktails and their non-alcoholic counterparts. There are no house wines in the generic sense; every bottle on the list reflects a relationship with a specific maker and a specific philosophy. The food menu, seasonal and built around biodynamic produce from neighbours and trusted growers, is designed to share and changes with what the market offers.

The name comes from the ancient Greek concept of leisure — not idleness, but the kind of engaged, unhurried attention that makes creativity possible. That framing is not decorative. On Tuesday evenings, Scholé becomes Tachinomi, a standing bar modelled on Tokyo's izakaya culture, walk-ins only, snacks and drinks, no bookings taken. The rest of the week runs on reservations. It is a wine bar and restaurant that has worked out what it wants to be and committed to it, which in Hobart's dining scene is rarer than it sounds. Come on a Tuesday if you want to understand what the place is actually about.
pullQuote: The list rewards curiosity — every wine on it is there for a reason.

name: Sen Vineyard
description: Sen Vineyard opens on a Sunday morning, from nine until noon, and that restraint tells you something. Set on 50 acres at White Hills in the upper Tamar Valley, the property operates primarily as a wedding and event venue — a contemporary estate with wrap-around glass walls, marble dining tables, and accommodation for six. It sits 15 minutes from Launceston, close enough to the city to be convenient, far enough to feel genuinely removed from it.

The vineyard is working, not decorative, though the website is quiet on specifics: no grape varieties listed, no tasting notes, no vintage information made publicly available. What is clear is that wine is part of the estate's offering — served at events, present on the property — rather than the primary draw. Visitors hoping for a dedicated tasting room or cellar door experience will find something more conditional here. The wines exist in the context of an occasion rather than as a destination in their own right.

The estate is pitched squarely at couples and event planners, with Matson Catering of Invermay as its preferred culinary partner. The 5.0 Google rating across 38 reviews suggests the experience delivers on its promises, at least for that audience. For a wine traveller, Sen Vineyard is best understood as a property where the vineyard provides atmosphere and provenance rather than the centrepiece. If you are attending a wedding here, the setting will likely impress. If you are driving out on a Sunday morning hoping to taste Tamar Valley Pinot Noir at a cellar door, call ahead — or reconsider the itinerary entirely.
pullQuote: The vineyard is real, but the cellar door experience is not really the point.

name: Sinapius Wines
description: The driveway into Sinapius tells you something before you reach the cellar door. The vineyard is planted with a specificity that reflects years of deliberate study — particular clones matched to particular blocks, nothing accidental about the layout. It is a small property in the Pipers Brook wine region of northern Tasmania, just above the banks of Pipers Brook itself, and it carries the focused energy of a place where decisions were made carefully and held to.

Sinapius works with the cool-climate varieties that northern Tasmania does best, with Pinot Noir and Chardonnay at the heart of the range. The winemaking philosophy is minimal intervention — not as a marketing position, but as a founding principle. The ambition from the beginning was to do most of the real work in the vineyard and let that work speak in the glass. The wines are not showy. They are precise, restrained, and site-specific in the way that serious Tasmanian wine tends to be when the producer genuinely believes in the region rather than just operating within it.

Linda Morice and her late husband Vaughn Dell bought the original two-hectare property in 2005, both Tasmania-born, returning after years in the Hunter Valley, Yarra Valley, and Margaret River. Vaughn died suddenly in May 2020, aged 39, leaving Linda to continue building what they had started together. She has. The name Sinapius traces to Linda's own family history — an ancestor, Isabella Sinapius, whose father migrated from Prussia in the early 1800s. That lineage matters here; this is a winery with a genuine sense of its own identity, not one assembled to fit a category. A 4.9 Google rating across nearly a hundred reviews is, in this case, a fair reflection of what visitors actually find.
pullQuote: These are wines made by someone with something to prove and the vineyard to back it up.

name: Small Wonder Wines
description: Small Wonder's winery building stops you before you've tasted a thing. Completed for vintage 2024 and designed by Kerry Hill Architects, it reworks the Tasmanian farm shed into three gabled forms running east to west — native spotted gum, recycled steel, local construction. It doesn't announce itself loudly. That restraint turns out to be the point.

Winemaker Ockie Myburgh came to Small Wonder after senior roles at Bay of Fires and, most recently, as Chief Winemaker at Josef Chromy — two of Tasmania's better-known addresses — so he arrives with a precise read on what the Tamar Valley can do. Picking decisions here are driven by flavour and acidity rather than sugar ripeness, which keeps alcohol lower and holds onto the kind of site detail that warmer harvests tend to erase. Fruit is hand-harvested from organically farmed vineyards across the Tamar and Coal River Valleys, then vinified in separate small parcels — sorted by clone, rootstock, soil type, vine vigour — in stainless steel, oak, and concrete. The sparkling wines are among their most decorated, and the 45-minute Vin Explorer winery tour is built around them. New oak is used, but sparingly. The winemaking team, which includes assistant winemaker Sara Dooley — formerly of Gusbourne in England and House of Arras — consistently errs on the side of less.

The winery itself is a recent arrival, but the philosophy behind it isn't. Earlier vintages were made under custom crush arrangements at Josef Chromy, which is actually where Myburgh first worked alongside the Small Wonder team. Bringing production fully on site in 2024 allowed for gravity-fed handling, tighter environmental controls, and solar and rainwater systems that reduce the winery's resource draw considerably. The 4.7-star Google rating across nearly 150 reviews suggests the visitor experience has kept pace with the winemaking ambition. For anyone serious about cool-climate Tasmanian wine, this is now a stop worth planning around rather than slotting in.
pullQuote: Ockie Myburgh's background shows — these are precise, composed wines that don't overreach.

name: Society Salamanca
description: Society Salamanca is not a winery. It is a cocktail bar on Montpelier Retreat in Battery Point, Hobart — and an unusually good one. Founded in 2015 by Angela Nichols and Emma Davis, it operates underground, off the main Salamanca strip, and the difference in atmosphere is immediate. The room is spacious and deliberately styled: industrial bones, luxurious fit-out, and a backbar running floor to ceiling with over 500 spirits, accessed by a sliding library ladder.

The drinks program is built around Tasmanian producers. The gin and whisky selections are the real draw — guests can order tasting platters of four Tasmanian gins, served with matching garnishes and house-made tonic water, or work through a whisky flight comparing local single malts side by side. The cocktail list is long and leans heavily on the island's distilleries, though the bartenders will make whatever you want. Cool-climate Tasmanian wines, ciders, and other local products round out the list for those not chasing spirits.

Nichols brought years of Hobart hospitality experience to the project; Davis came from a legal career and wanted something different. Together they have built something with genuine staying power — Society Salamanca holds a 4.7 rating across nearly 400 reviews, which in a city with no shortage of good bars means something. They also run Raincheck Lounge and Willing Wine Bar in North Hobart. The venue is women-owned, LGBTQ+-friendly, and hosts regular events including drag bingo and disco nights. If you want a structured tasting of what Tasmania's distillers are producing, this is the most efficient and most enjoyable place in Hobart to do it.
pullQuote: The gin tasting platter alone justifies the trip down those stairs.

name: Sonny
description: Sonny occupies a narrow room at 120a Elizabeth Street, Hobart — about twenty stools, ten at the bar, the rest at small tables — and has operated on walk-ins only since it opened in winter 2019. The first pair of Pitt & Giblin SuperWax Mini speakers in any public venue sit at one end of the room. Vinyl only. Someone flips the record when the side ends.

The wine list is built around discovery. The house feature is mystery wine: tell the staff what you're after, and they choose for you, factoring in the food you've ordered and the weather outside. It works because the list is genuinely considered rather than decorative. Tasmania produces some of Australia's most distinctive cool-climate sparkling, Pinot Noir, and Chardonnay, and a bar this focused tends to carry bottles you won't find anywhere obvious. Specific selections change, but the orientation is toward producers working carefully and at small scale.

The food menu is limited by design — no full kitchen — but a classic night at Sonny runs oysters from Tas Oyster Co, a toast dish, pasta, and cheese from Tongola Farm. It changes with what local producers have available and with whoever is cooking. The place closed on Tuesdays and Wednesdays from the start, takes a week off at Easter and Christmas, and has never tried to be anything larger than it is. That consistency, five years in, is its own kind of statement. For anyone arriving in Hobart without a dinner reservation, it is the most reliable good decision you can make after 4pm.
pullQuote: Order the mystery wine — they're almost always right about what you actually want.

name: South Wine Bar
description: South sits at 4 Cascade Road in South Hobart, a small, two-room bar that fills up fast enough to make the booking advice feel less like a suggestion and more like a warning. Kathie and Jeremy Waterhouse run the place themselves, and it shows — the kind of venue where the specials board reflects what someone was actually experimenting with that week, not what the season demands in the abstract.

The drinks list is built almost entirely around Tasmanian producers, covering wine, spirits, and non-alcoholic options. When something from outside the state makes it on, the implication is clear: it had to earn its place. The wine focus is regional by conviction rather than novelty, which in Tasmania means cool-climate varieties — expect Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, and Riesling to anchor the list, drawn from producers across the island. The list rotates with the same logic as the kitchen: what's interesting right now, not what's permanently on theme.

Jezz and Kathie opened South with a clear enough brief — good food, no pretension, genuinely local — and the 4.9 Google rating across 111 reviews suggests they've landed it. The menu changes with available produce, plates are sized for sharing, and pizza on a thin crispy base runs Friday through Sunday. It's open Wednesday to Saturday from 3:30 PM, closed on public holidays, and draws a crowd that treats it less like a destination and more like a standing arrangement. For visitors, the honest appeal is this: it's one of the few places in Hobart where the Tasmanian wine list has been assembled with real editorial judgment rather than regional obligation.
pullQuote: The Tasmanian wine list here has been curated, not just collected.

name: Stefano Lubiana Wines
description: At Granton on the Derwent River, Stefano Lubiana's 28-hectare estate runs on a different clock than most. Planting schedules follow cosmic calendars. Treatments are drawn from the landscape, not a supplier's catalogue. The cellar door might have a Malvasia open, or a grappa tucked behind the bench. It's a place where the unexpected is a feature of the program, not an accident.

The estate is planted predominantly to Pinot Noir and Chardonnay, with sparkling wines rounding out the core range. Winemaking here is minimal-intervention in a serious sense: wild yeasts, whole-bunch fermentation, no synthetic additives or pesticides. The biodynamic certification, held since 2010, is the oldest in Tasmania. The Pinot Noirs have won Best Biodynamic Wine in the World at the London International Wine Challenge on three separate occasions — not once as a fluke, but three times as a pattern. That record belongs to a small number of producers globally, and Lubiana is one of them.

Steve Lubiana is sixth-generation. His father Mario made wine in South Australia; before that, the family worked vines in Italy. Steve and his wife Monique established the Granton site in 1990, and their son Marco has since joined the operation. Thirty-five years in, the estate reads as a coherent project rather than an accumulation of decisions — biodynamics, sparkling wine, Burgundian varieties, minimal intervention, all pulling in the same direction. For a visitor, that coherence is what registers most. This is a winery with a clear idea of what it is, and the track record to back it up.
pullQuote: Three Best Biodynamic Wine in the World titles don't come from good intentions alone.

name: StelaVino Wine Tours
description: StelaVino is not a winery — it is a guided wine tour, and that distinction matters. Steliano Cusmiani, who goes by Stel, picks you up in Hobart and spends a full day taking you through Tasmania's cellar doors. The pace is deliberate. The itinerary is shaped by decades of industry knowledge, not a marketing brief.

Because StelaVino is a tour operation rather than a producer, the wines on any given day depend on which doors Stel opens. What is consistent is the lens through which you encounter them. Stel has worked across the Australian wine industry for over 45 years — at Yalumba, Lindeman's, Seppelt's, McWilliam's, and Orlando — and was mentored by Len Evans, one of the most formative figures in Australian wine education. He ran fine wine sales for Seppelt's across New South Wales before moving into management and education. That background means you are not getting a rehearsed tasting-room script. You are getting someone who can read a wine and tell you why it matters.

Stel's entry into wine started in 1976 at his uncle's restaurant in New York, where a young chef taught him to take the glass seriously. He has been in the industry ever since. StelaVino itself grew from that lifetime of experience and a move to Tasmania, where he found a region with genuine complexity worth explaining. In November 2021 the operation won the Tasmanian Hospitality Association's Best Guided Tour in Tasmania award — a hospitality prize, not a wine medal, which is actually the right category. The tours run Monday through Friday only, by appointment. For a visitor who wants to understand Tasmanian wine rather than just drink it, a day with Stel is a more efficient use of time than any self-guided cellar door crawl.
pullQuote: Forty-five years in the trade means he knows which questions to ask before you even think to ask them.

name: Stone & Flame
description: Stone & Flame operates out of Hobart as an evening-only destination — open five to nine, Tuesday through Saturday, closed Sundays — which tells you something about what it is before you walk through the door. This is not a cellar door in any conventional sense. The hours are a restaurant's hours, and the fire-driven cooking that defines the kitchen shapes the entire experience, including how the wines are chosen and served.

Because no winery under this name appears in Tasmania's wine registry or any producer database, Stone & Flame is best understood as a wine-forward dining venue rather than a producing winery. The list almost certainly leans into Tasmania's cool-climate strengths — Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, Riesling, and sparkling wines from the Tamar Valley, Coal River, and Huon Valley — curated to hold their own against food built around heat and char. What a venue like this does well, when it does it well, is make the wine list feel like a continuation of the cooking rather than a footnote to it.

Hobart's dining scene has shifted considerably in the past decade, and Stone & Flame sits within that evolution — a city increasingly confident in what its island produces and willing to put it front and centre. The 4.9 rating across its reviews, however limited in number, suggests the experience lands consistently. For visitors, the evening-only format means this is a destination you plan for rather than stumble into. If you are in Hobart and serious about eating and drinking well in the same sitting, that kind of focus is usually worth respecting.
pullQuote: The hours alone tell you this place is serious about dinner, not drop-ins.

name: Stoney Rise Wine Company
description: The cellar door at Stoney Rise is open Thursday through Sunday, and that limited schedule tells you something: this is a working vineyard first. The property sits less than a kilometre from the Tamar River, at elevations between 10 and 30 metres above sea level — low, close to the water, with the kind of riparian influence that shapes a growing season quietly but persistently. The vines were planted in 1986. By the time Joe and Lou Holyman bought the place in 2004, those vines already had nearly two decades of root depth behind them.

The focus is Pinot Noir and Chardonnay, which account for the bulk of the 7.35 hectares under vine. More recently, the Holymans have added Trousseau — 0.7 hectares — and a small parcel of Grüner Veltliner, which is rare enough in Tasmania to be worth seeking out on its own. Crop loads are kept tight, averaging 1.5 bunches per shoot across the vineyard, with weaker vines carrying just one. Herbicides have been off the table for close to a decade. Biodynamic preparations are now being introduced to build soil health. The winemaking philosophy reads as deliberate restraint: the farming is precise, the intervention minimal, and the vine age does most of the talking.

Joe Holyman comes from a Tasmanian wine family — his relatives have been growing grapes in the state since 1984. Before returning to Tasmania, he bought wine for The Wine Society's 50,000 Australian members, which gave him a clear picture of what serious cool-climate wine looks like at scale. Lou ran operations at South Australian wineries before taking on the business side at Stoney Rise. Their on-site winery, built in 2008, completed what the 2004 purchase had set in motion. The result is a property with genuine continuity — same site, older vines, incremental refinement. For visitors who care about what vine age actually does to a wine, this is a place that makes that argument clearly.
pullQuote: The Trousseau alone is reason enough to rearrange your itinerary through the Tamar Valley.

name: Swinging Gate Vineyard
description: The cellar door at Swinging Gate was once a machinery shed. That conversion matters — it tells you something about how this place operates. There is no performance of elegance here, just a working property in the upper Tamar Valley that has been quietly brought back to life, and a tasting space where the wines speak for themselves.

The vines were planted more than thirty years ago, and age shows in the glass. Swinging Gate works with cool-climate Tamar Valley fruit under a minimal intervention philosophy — the goal being to let old-vine character carry the wine rather than mask it. The Tamar Valley's combination of cool nights and strong daytime sun pushes toward wines with genuine tension: ripeness held in check by acidity. Without more specific vintage data to hand, it would be misleading to single out individual bottles, but the through-line across the range is structure over fruit-forwardness, restraint over extraction. These are wines that reward attention.

The vineyard sat dormant for fifteen years before Doug, Corrie, and their team purchased it in 2014 and began the slow work of restoration. That gap is not a footnote — it means the vines were already mature when the current owners inherited them, and the intervening decade of careful management has only deepened that resource. Reopening under a new identity, they rebuilt the operation from the ground up without abandoning what the site had already accumulated. A 4.9 rating across nearly 370 reviews suggests visitors notice the difference between a winery finding its feet and one that has grown into itself. The upper Tamar Valley has no shortage of cellar doors, but few of them are pouring fruit from vines this old.
pullQuote: Old vines, minimal intervention — the Tamar Valley in its most honest form.

name: Tamar Ridge Cellar Door
description: Tamar Ridge sits at the more polished end of the Tamar Valley cellar door experience. The tasting room is open daily, the staff are unhurried, and the setup invites you to sit with a glass rather than move quickly through a flight. For a producer operating at this scale, it retains a sense of proportion — not overwhelming, not precious.

Winemaker Tom Wallace concentrates his efforts on the varieties that the Tamar Valley handles best. Pinot Noir is the clear focus — the cool climate and long growing season here produce fruit that builds structure gradually, and Wallace works toward balance and site expression rather than extraction. Chardonnay and Riesling round out the core range, both made with the kind of restraint that suits the valley's character. The winemaking approach leans minimal in intervention, letting the vineyard do the initial work. What ends up in the glass is clean and precise, with the lean fruit profile that distinguishes Tasmanian wine from mainland cool-climate counterparts.

Tamar Ridge is one of the Tamar Valley's more established names — a producer that has grown into a regional anchor without losing focus on what made it worth paying attention to in the first place. It occupies an interesting position: large enough to have genuine distribution and recognition, careful enough to still produce wines that reflect where they come from. The slow lunch offering, priced at $110 per adult, is a serious commitment to the cellar door as destination rather than just retail stop. For visitors who want a single address that captures the Tamar Valley's range and ambition in one sitting, this is a sensible place to begin.
pullQuote: The Pinot Noir here has the structure to age — most Tasmanian bottles at this price don't.

name: Tamar Valley Wine Centre
description: Tamar Valley Wine Centre sits at 49 Main Road in Exeter, functioning as something between a bottle shop, a bar, and a meeting point for the valley's producers. It's open seven days from nine in the morning until nine at night — hours that suit both the lunch crowd and the people passing through after a long day on the wine trail. The setup is deliberately accessible: you can taste, buy, or sit down, without having to commit to a formal cellar door experience.

The Centre doesn't produce its own wines, which is the point. The shelves and the pours are drawn from local Tamar Valley vineyards, brewers, and makers — a curated cross-section of the region rather than a single producer's range. That means visitors can move across Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, Riesling, and sparkling from multiple estates in a single sitting. For those new to Tasmanian wine, it functions as a useful introduction; for those already familiar with the region, it offers a way to compare and contrast without driving between cellar doors. What's on pour will shift with the seasons and the producers they're currently backing.

The Centre positions itself as a regional hub rather than a destination winery, which gives it a different kind of usefulness. It supports local growers and makers directly, and its staff — described as a small, hands-on team across the bar, kitchen, and floor — seem genuinely invested in the place. With a 4.5 rating across 265 Google reviews, it holds up in practice. For visitors trying to get a broad read on Tamar Valley producers before committing to individual cellar doors, or for locals who want a reliable place to eat and drink in Exeter, this is the most practical stop in the area.
pullQuote: If you want to understand Tamar Valley producers quickly, this is where you start.

name: Tamar Valley Wine Tours
description: Tamar Valley Wine Tours is not a winery — it is a guided entry point into a region that rewards local knowledge. Gillian and Terry, both Launceston locals, run small groups of eight on average, eleven at most, through the Tamar Valley's cellar doors. That ceiling matters. It keeps the day from becoming a shuffle through crowds, and it means the people running your tour can actually talk to you.

Because this is a tour operation rather than a producer, the wines vary by visit — whatever the valley's cellar doors are pouring that day. The Tamar Valley's strength lies in cool-climate varieties: Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, Riesling, and Pinot Gris dominate the region's output, with producers ranging from well-established names to smaller growers who rarely appear on mainland shelves. The tour's value is in access and context — a guide who knows which vineyards are worth your attention and can explain why a Tamar Riesling behaves differently from one grown further south near the Huon Valley.

Gillian and Terry have built Launceston's top-rated wine tour on a straightforward premise: keep groups small, include a proper lunch with wine, and run the day at a pace that allows for real conversation. The operation is flexible enough that a solo traveller can still book — their Discovery Tour runs with a single guest, which is unusual and worth noting. A 100% refund guarantee is offered, and pickups run from Launceston or the Hotel Grand Chancellor. For visitors who want to cover the Tamar Valley seriously without spending the day navigating it themselves, this is the most direct route in.
pullQuote: The small group size is the whole point — it changes what the day actually feels like.

name: The Den Salamanca
description: The Den sits in Hobart's Salamanca precinct and runs late — until 3 AM on weekends — which immediately sets it apart from the cellar-door-and-cheese-board circuit. It is a bar first, with a kitchen and a serious cocktail program, and it makes no apologies for that. The room draws a crowd that stays.

The wine program here is not the point, and the venue is upfront about that. The emphasis falls on cocktails built around Tasmanian ingredients, and on a food menu that references local produce. If you are looking for a flight of cool-climate Pinot Noir or a deep back-catalogue of Tasmanian Riesling, this is not the address. What you will find is a drinks list that takes the island's larder seriously, framed within a hospitality operation that holds a 4.5-star rating across more than 1,100 Google reviews — which suggests it is doing something consistently right.

The Den positions itself around a defined idea: that Tasmania's geographic isolation and clean-air cool climate produce ingredients worth treating with some intention. That philosophy shows up more in the cocktail menu than in any wine selection, and visitors who arrive expecting a conventional winery experience will need to recalibrate. What the venue offers instead is a late-night version of Tasmanian produce culture — jalapeño poppers, skewers, margaritas, and the kind of room where the night runs longer than you planned. For visitors already in Salamanca and looking for somewhere that stays open and stays interesting, it fills a gap that most cellar doors in the region simply cannot.
pullQuote: Come for the cocktails and the kitchen; don't come expecting a wine list.

name: Timbre Kitchen
description: Timbre Kitchen sits on the Velo Wines vineyard in the West Tamar — one of the first vineyards planted in the valley — and operates out of what feels less like a restaurant than a room where someone's neighbor might drop off cucumbers through the back door. That actually happens here. Menus are written daily, driven by whatever backyard growers and local producers bring in that morning. The wood-fired oven runs through service. It is open Thursday to Sunday, and closes Monday through Wednesday without apology.

The wine list centers on Velo Wines, whose vines have been in the Tamar Valley long enough to matter. Vineyard management falls to Rod Thorpe, a local with deep regional roots, and the winemaking is a collaboration between Thorpe and Matthias Utzinger. The wines have been selling out before the following vintage arrives — a reliable sign that the market has caught up with what's in the glass. Beyond Velo, the list draws from across Tasmania and the mainland, with enough range to reward both the curious and the committed.

Chef and owner Matt Adams opened Timbre in 2016 after years in Launceston kitchens — Fee & Me, Pierre's, Josef Chromy Wines — and before that, an apprenticeship in Queensland. He has won best dessert at Festivale three times. The venue has been covered in Gourmet Traveller, Qantas Magazine, Lonely Planet, and named among Australia's best restaurants. Adams works closely with Launceston's UNESCO City of Gastronomy designation and a network of regional food festivals. None of that explains the 4.9 Google rating across 358 reviews as well as the simple fact that the food is cooked by someone who grew up here and hasn't stopped paying attention to the place.
pullQuote: Velo's wines are selling out before next vintage — that tells you most of what you need to know.

name: Tolpuddle Vineyard
description: Twenty hectares of mature vines on a single even slope, lean soils underfoot, forest at the top and water at the bottom — Tolpuddle is the kind of site that explains itself the moment you step out of the car. The Coal River Valley, half an hour from Hobart, sits in the rain shadow of Tasmania's western ranges, which keeps it dry and cold in ways that most mainland Australian wine regions are not. That combination shows in the fruit.

Tolpuddle makes two wines: a Chardonnay and a Pinot Noir, both single-vineyard, both from vines planted in 1988. The Chardonnay has won the Françoise De Castella Trophy — awarded to the best young white wine at the Melbourne Royal Wine Awards — three times, for the 2018, 2021, and 2023 vintages. Trophies at the International Wine Challenge have followed. The style reflects the site: the cold, dry climate builds flavour slowly and holds acidity without intervention, so the wines don't need to be pushed. What comes through is precision rather than weight.

The vineyard was established in 1988 by Tony Jordan, Gary Crittenden, and Bill Casimaty. Michael Hill-Smith AM MW and Martin Shaw of Shaw + Smith acquired it in 2011 — not through any strategic search, but through a single unplanned visit during what was meant to be a reconnaissance trip. Hill-Smith has described driving through the gate as love at first sight, which is the kind of claim that usually signals sentimentality, but the subsequent track record makes it difficult to argue with. The cellar door is open Monday and Friday through Sunday. Given how small the annual production is, visiting in person remains the most reliable way to actually get hold of the wines.
pullQuote: The Chardonnay is as good an argument for Tasmanian cool-climate wine as anything being made right now.

name: Trophy Room
description: Trophy Room occupies a converted space on Argyle Street in North Hobart — dinner hours only through most of the week, with lunch service added Thursday through Saturday. It is the kind of place that keeps a tight schedule and expects you to book ahead. That discipline shows in how the room runs.

The available information doesn't detail a house wine list tied to an estate vineyard, which suggests Trophy Room operates as a curated wine bar rather than a producer-cellar door. In that context, what matters is the selection and the knowledge behind it — Tasmania's cool-climate canon of Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, and Riesling from producers across the Derwent Valley and Coal River would be the natural backbone of any serious Hobart list. A 4.8 rating across 210 reviews is a reliable signal that the pours are landing. Whether that means deep Tasmanian coverage or a broader Australian and international range, this is a place where the wine list has clearly been thought through rather than assembled.

North Hobart's strip on Argyle Street has become one of the more consistent dining precincts in southern Tasmania, and Trophy Room fits the neighbourhood's rhythm — serious enough to draw regulars, relaxed enough not to announce itself. The Tuesday evening opening is a recent addition, which points to a business building its audience rather than coasting on an established name. For visitors to Hobart, the Tuesday and Wednesday availability makes it a useful option when many of the city's better tables go quiet mid-week. Book early, and go with the expectation that the room knows what it's doing.
pullQuote: A 4.8 across 210 reviews in Hobart doesn't happen by accident — this room earns it.

name: Velo Wines
description: The oldest Cabernet Sauvignon vines in Tasmania are here, at Legana, planted in 1966 when almost no one else in the state was taking the grape seriously. Walking the property, that history is legible in the vineyard itself — old wood, established rows, a site that has been producing continuously for over half a century.

Velo grows around seven varieties across the Tamar Valley site, including Pinot Noir, Riesling, Chardonnay, Pinot Gris, Sauvignon Blanc, and Shiraz alongside the heritage Cabernet Sauvignon. Annual fruit production sits at approximately 15 tonnes, kept deliberately small, with all 2024 vintage grapes grown onsite. The winery picked up four bronze and two silver medals at the 2019 Tasmanian Wine Show — modest enough not to oversell, solid enough to confirm the wines are honest. Rod Thorpe, who previously founded Moore's Hill, manages the vineyard and has maintained yield quality through that transition. The cool-climate Tamar Valley site does most of the talking in the wines.

The original estate, Chateau Legana, was founded in 1966 by Graham Wiltshire and Michael Curtis — early movers in Tasmanian wine at a time when the industry barely existed. In 2001, Michael and Mary Wilson rebranded it as Velo Wines, the name a nod to Michael's career as an Olympic cyclist. In late 2024, new ownership arrived in the form of a Sydney-based surgeon with stated intentions to build on rather than remake what's already here. The continuity matters: Thorpe remains on as vineyard manager, and the founding vines are still in the ground. For a visitor, the combination of the oldest Cab Sauv vines in Tasmania and an unbroken site history since 1966 makes this a stop with genuine substance behind it.
pullQuote: The Cabernet vines alone make it worth the detour — nothing else in Tasmania comes close for age.

name: Viridian Wines
description: Broadview Vineyard sits two hectares small on sloping tertiary limestone rubble above the Derwent River, about twenty kilometres north of Hobart. Its neighbours are Stefano Lubiana and Derwent Estate. The valley fogs that roll in from the wetlands below are not a marketing detail — they shape what ends up in the glass.

Viridian grows Riesling, Pinot Noir, and Chardonnay, producing both still and sparkling wines from single-vineyard fruit. Winemaking is handled by Alain Rousseau at Frogmore Creek, with minimal intervention the stated goal — the intention being to let seasonal and site character drive the wine rather than the cellar. Hand pruning and hand harvesting are standard practice here, and fruit is picked at what the family considers optimum ripeness rather than to a schedule. The volumes are genuinely limited, which in this case is a function of the vineyard's size rather than a selling point.

The Rochford family planted their first vines in 1996 with no generational background in wine. What they brought instead was deliberate study — Lyn Rochford holds a postgraduate qualification in Viticulture and Wine Technology — and a specific commitment to environmentally sustainable practice that predates the industry's wider embrace of the term. The winery's name comes from viridian green, a pigment developed in the 1850s as a non-toxic alternative to earlier arsenic-based greens. That origin story is not incidental; it shapes how the family thinks about what they're doing on the land. With fifteen-plus years of production and a vineyard that has reached its natural ceiling at two hectares, this is a small operation that has found its scale and is working within it. Visitors looking for a tasting-room spectacle will need to adjust expectations. Those who want to understand what a two-hectare limestone block above the Derwent actually tastes like will find it here.
pullQuote: The Riesling in particular shows what that limestone rubble above the Derwent can do.

name: Westella Vineyard
description: Westella operates on a small parcel of land near the Tamar River's northern reach, close enough to kanamaluka that the river moderates the frosts that trouble other growers in the valley. The cellar door opens Thursday through Sunday. That's the full schedule — and it suits a place that has never been interested in doing several things at once.

Westella grows one variety: Pinot Noir. From that single commitment comes a focused range — a Pinot Noir, a White Pinot, a Pinot Rosé, a Vintage Rosé, a Blanc de Noir, and two named wines, Tippogoree and Clairmarais, alongside a wine called Restore Pedder. The entire lineup is an exercise in what one grape can do across different expressions rather than a survey of the region's possibilities. There are no Rieslings here, no Chardonnays hedging the bet. The winemaking approach reads as deliberate restraint: grow one thing well, understand it deeply, and find its range.

The founders came to Westella after years of asking the same question whenever they visited other growers: what do you grow? The answers were always plural — a bit of this, a few acres of that. Eventually they recognised what was bothering them. They had always been singular in their thinking, and the vineyard needed to match that. Tasmania was the only place they had considered, and Pinot was the only variety. Those two decisions made everything else follow. A 5.0 rating across 42 Google reviews suggests visitors tend to find what they came for. For anyone who has grown tired of wineries that spread themselves thin, Westella is a direct counter-argument.
pullQuote: When a winery commits to one variety this completely, the wines have nowhere to hide — and these don't need to.

name: Willie Smith's Apple Shed
description: The Apple Shed sits on 115 acres along the Huon Highway in Grove, thirty minutes south of Hobart, in a valley that once shipped apples to markets across the globe. The building itself is what it sounds like — a working property repurposed for visitors, not a cellar door built from scratch to look like one. That difference shows in how the place feels.

Willie Smith's makes cider, not wine — and it's worth being direct about that. The focus is organic apple cider, produced from fruit grown on the estate, alongside apple brandy, Pommeau, and a Blanco spirit developed through their distilling program. The organic certification isn't incidental; the farm explicitly avoids synthetic inputs, and the ciders reflect that restraint. The range spans from approachable sparkling styles to more considered, complex expressions. Recent awards suggest the distilling side is drawing as much attention as the cider itself.

Four generations of the Smith family have worked this orchard, and that continuity shapes everything about the operation — including the decision, at some point in the recent past, to stop treating the farm purely as a production asset and open it to visitors properly. The Huon Valley's apple industry contracted sharply over the latter half of the twentieth century; Willie Smith's is one of the more visible examples of a producer finding a durable path through that contraction. Friday nights extend to 9pm, the kitchen runs most of the week, and the 4.6-star rating across well over a thousand reviews suggests the experience holds up to repeat visitors. For anyone making the drive south from Hobart, it earns its place on the itinerary.
pullQuote: The apple brandy alone justifies the drive down the Huon Highway.

name: Willing Wine Bar
description: Willing Wine Bar is the kind of North Hobart spot that earns its reputation through consistency rather than concept. Open Tuesday through Saturday from late afternoon, it runs a tight operation — small room, warm service, no pretension. People arrive for a glass and end up staying for dinner. That's not an accident.

The wine list doesn't anchor itself to a single producer or style. It rotates, which means the by-the-glass selection reflects what's interesting right now rather than what was locked in at the start of the year. The bottle list runs deeper for those who want to settle in. Willing doesn't appear to make its own wine — this is a bar, not a cellar door — but the curation suggests someone paying genuine attention to what Tasmania and the broader natural wine world are producing. If you're after a specific label, call ahead. If you're happy to be guided, you're in good hands.

Willing opened in North Hobart as a neighbourhood wine bar with a food program serious enough to hold its own. The menu is short and considered — croquetas, fish crudo, pasta alla vodka, steak frites, local oysters. Classic wine bar logic: dishes that make you want another glass. A 4.7 rating across more than 200 Google reviews points to a place that delivers reliably, not just on opening night. For visitors to Hobart without a car and a cellar door itinerary, Willing offers something genuinely useful: a concentrated hit of Tasmanian wine culture, curated by people who clearly care what ends up in your glass.
pullQuote: The rotating by-the-glass list is doing the work most wine bars won't bother with.

name: Wineries & Vineyards
description: The name 'Wineries & Vineyards' signals something categorical rather than particular, and that is precisely the problem: no single property by this name operates in Tasmania. What the search returns instead is a region — one of Australia's smallest and most distinctive wine-producing areas — whose individual producers each have sharply different identities, histories, and styles worth knowing on their own terms.

Tasmania's vineyards work almost exclusively with cool-climate varieties: Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, Riesling, and Pinot Gris dominate, alongside a sparkling wine sector that has drawn serious international attention. The island's naturally high acidity and long, slow growing seasons produce wines that read more like northern Europe than the Australian mainland — restrained, precise, built for the table rather than the trophy shelf. Producers like Tolpuddle, Moorilla, and Jansz have each demonstrated, in different ways, that the approach holds up against rigorous international comparison.

Modern Tasmanian winemaking traces to the mid-1950s, when Jean Miguet planted in the north and Claudio Alcorso established Moorilla near Hobart in 1958. The decade that followed saw Heemskerk, Pipers Brook, and Jansz take shape in the northeast, and a Heemskerk Cabernet from 1976 — gold at the Royal Melbourne Show — gave the island its first moment of national recognition. The region now spans Coal River Valley, Derwent Valley, Huon Valley, and the Tamar, each with producers worth visiting on their own merits. Any serious trip to Tasmania starts by picking one of them specifically.
pullQuote: Tasmania rewards the visitor who arrives with a specific winery in mind, not a general itinerary.
